accusative-case.title: Unit 5: The Accusative Case & Basic Prepositions
accusative-case.content: Unit 5: The Accusative Case & Basic Prepositions In Unit 2, you learned that when a noun is the Object (receiving the action), it drops its final -ς (if masculine) and its article becomes τον, την, or το . IMPORTANT The Masculine 'N' We always keep the final -ν in the masculine article ( τον, στον, έναν ) in writing to avoid confusion with the neuter article ( το, στο, ένα ). This is true even if the next word starts with a soft sound! Now, we will see the Accusative Case in action through verbs and the "Big Three" prepositions. Unit 5: The Accusative Case & Basic Prepositions In Unit 2, you learned that when a noun is the Object (receiving the action), it drops its final -ς (if masculine) and its article becomes τον, την, or το . IMPORTANT The Masculine 'N' We always keep the final -ν in the masculine article ( τον, στον, έναν ) in writing to avoid confusion with the neuter article ( το, στο, ένα ). This is true even if the next word starts with a soft sound! Now, we will see the Accusative Case in action through verbs and the "Big Three" prepositions. 1. Verbs that use the Accusative Most "action" (transitive) verbs require the direct object to be in the Accusative case. Βλέπω τον Γιώργο. (I see George). Έχω την τσάντα. (I have the bag). Διαβάζω το βιβλίο. (I read the book). CAUTION The "Mirror" Verb Remember: The verb Είμαι (I am) is special. It acts as a linking verb (a mirror), so it takes a "subject predicate" and keeps everything in the Nominative case (ο, η, το). Είμαι ο Γιώργος. (I am George). Η Μαρία είναι γιατρός. (Mary is a doctor). 2. The Big Three Prepositions (σε, από, με) In Greek, almost all basic prepositions force the noun phrase that follows them into the Accusative case. A. Σε (To / At / In / On) This word shows location in space or time, as well as direction. In Greek, we almost never say "σε" and "the" separately. The preposition σε obligatorily merges with the definite article into one word: σε + τον = στον σε + την = στη(ν) σε + το = στο (And for plurals: στους, στις, στα) . Example: Πάω στο σινεμά. (I am going to the cinema). Example: Το πιάτο είναι στο τραπέζι. (The plate is on the table). B. Από (From / Since / By) Used for origins, time, cause, and the agent of an action. It does not merge with the article in writing, but the article must still be in the Accusative. Είμαι από την Ελλάδα. (I am from Greece). Έρχομαι από τον γιατρό. (I am coming from the doctor). TIP Blend it! Even though it's written separately, the final vowel of από is often optionally deleted before a definite article when speaking quickly. Από την Αθήνα sounds like "ap-tin Athina" (απ' την Αθήνα)! C. Με (With) Used for company, instruments, means of transport, or manner. Είμαι με τη Μαρία. (I am with Maria). Θέλω καφέ με ζάχαρη. (I want coffee with sugar). Θα πάμε με το λεωφορείο. (We will go by bus). 3. Pronunciation: The Dropping 'N' & The Blending Sounds While the masculine articles ( τον, στον, έναν ) always keep their -ν in writing, the feminine words ( την, στην, μιαν ) and negative particles ( δεν, μην ) are more selective. The Dropping 'N' Rule: They only keep the -ν if the next word starts with: 1. A vowel (α, ε, η, ι, ο, υ, ω) 2. A hard consonant (κ, π, τ, ξ, ψ) 3. A digraph (μπ, ντ, γκ, τσ, τζ) Otherwise, the 'ν' is normally deleted in both speech and writing. Στη δασκάλα (The teacher - drops before the soft 'δ') Στην πόρτα (The door - keeps before the hard 'π') The Blending Consonants Rule: When the final 'ν' (from either a masculine or feminine word) meets a hard consonant like π, τ, or κ , the sounds magically blend together! ν + π = mb: τον πατέρα sounds like to(m)-batéra . ν + τ = nd: τον τρελό sounds like to(n)-dreló . ν + κ = ng: τον κάλεσαν sounds like to(ng)-gálesan . (If you aren't sure, it is usually safer to include the -ν in speech—native speakers will still understand you perfectly!) 🎯 Unit 5 Mastery Checklist I can use βλέπω, έχω, and θέλω with the correct Accusative articles. I know that σε + την = στην , and can use στον, στο, στους, στις, στα . I remember to drop the -ς for masculine nouns after από and με . I know the rule for dropping or keeping the final -ν in feminine articles. I understand how the final -ν blends with hard consonants to make mb, nd, and ng sounds. Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Survival Phrases Up Next: Unit 6: Adjectives & Agreement

adjective-comparison.title: Unit 25: Adjective Comparison (Better, Faster)
adjective-comparison.content: Unit 25: Adjective Comparison (Better, Faster) In Greek, there are two ways to compare things (e.g., "tall" ➔ "taller"). You can use the easy "more" method or the specialized one-word "suffix" method. 1. The Easy Way: Πιο + Adjective This is currently the most common way to speak. Just put the word πιο (more) in front of the positive form of the adjective. Μεγάλος (Big) ➔ Πιο μεγάλος (Bigger) Ωραίος (Beautiful) ➔ Πιο ωραίος (More beautiful) How to say "than": To express the second term of a comparison (e.g., "taller than ..."), Greek uses the preposition από + Accusative case. Ο Γιώργος είναι πιο ψηλός από τον Νίκο. (George is taller than Nikos). TIP Native Secret: The Genitive Pronoun Instead of using από εμένα (than me) or από εσένα (than you), native speakers often just attach a weak possessive pronoun in the Genitive case directly after the comparative adjective! Η Άννα είναι μεγαλύτερή μου. (Anna is older than me / my senior). 2. The Native Way: The -τερος Suffix If you want to sound more precise or formal, you form a one-word comparative by adding the suffix -τερος, -τερη, -τερο to the stem of the adjective. The Stress Rule: Comparative forms with this suffix always have their stress on the third syllable from the end (the antepenultimate syllable). Ψηλός ➔ Ψηλότερος (Taller) Νέος (Young) ➔ Νεότερος (Younger) Σπουδαίος (Important) ➔ Σπουδαιότερος (More important) WARNING The "Πιο" Only Adjectives Not all adjectives can take the -τερος suffix! You MUST use the πιο method for: 1. Passive participles ending in -μένος (e.g., πιο κουρασμένος - more tired). 2. Adjectives ending in -ης, -ια, -ι or -ων, -ουσα, -ον . 3. Indeclinable colors like μπλε or ροζ (e.g., πιο μπλε ). 3. Relative Superlatives (The Best) To say "the most" or "the best," you are forming the Relative Superlative. You simply add the Definite Article ( ο, η, το ) in front of either of your comparative forms. Ο πιο καλός (The best / The most good) Ο καλύτερος (The better one / The best one) Το ψηλότερο κτήριο. (The tallest building). 4. The Absolute Superlative: -τατος If you want to stress an exceptional property, saying something is "extremely" or "very, very" X, use the absolute superlative suffix -τατος, -τατη, -τατο . Like the comparative, the stress always falls on the third syllable from the end. Ωραιότατος (Extremely beautiful / Most beautiful) Βαθύτατος (Very deep indeed / Profound) Φθηνότατος (Super cheap) 5. The Top Irregulars Like English ("good" ➔ "better", not "gooder"), the most common adjectives in Greek are irregular. You must memorize their comparative and absolute superlative forms: Positive Comparative (Better/More) Absolute Superlative (Extremely/Excellent) Καλός (Good) καλύτερος άριστος / κάλλιστος Κακός (Bad) χειρότερος χείριστος / κάκιστος Πολύς (Much/Many) περισσότερος πλείστος Μεγάλος (Big) μεγαλύτερος μέγιστος (Maximal) Μικρός (Small) μικρότερος ελάχιστος (Minimal) Λίγος (Little) λιγότερος (Less) ελάχιστος (Extremely little) 🎯 Unit 25 Mastery Checklist I can use πιο + adjective to compare things easily. I recognize the -τερος suffix and remember to stress it on the third syllable from the end. I know how to use από + Accusative or a Genitive weak pronoun for "than". I know that adding the definite article ( ο, η, το ) creates the "most" (Relative Superlative). I can use the -τατος suffix to say something is "extremely" X. I have memorized the irregulars ( καλύτερος , χειρότερος , περισσότερος , μεγαλύτερος ). Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Adjectives & Comparisons Up Next: Unit 26: Participles (-οντας, -μένος)

adjectives.title: Unit 6: Adjectives, Colors & Numbers
adjectives.content: Unit 6: Adjectives, Colors & Numbers In English, adjectives (describing words) are simple: the word "good" never changes. In Greek, adjectives are chameleons . They must perfectly match the noun they describe in Gender , Number , and Case . We call this Agreement . 1. The Chameleon Rule Every Greek adjective in the dictionary has three versions (Masculine, Feminine, Neuter). Notice how the endings often "rhyme" with the noun! Focus Masculine (-ος) Feminine (-η/-α) Neuter (-ο) Example Ο καλός πατέρας Η καλή μητέρα Το καλό παιδί Logic The good father The good mother The good child When you use an adjective as an object (Accusative Case), it must flex with the noun. Subject: Ο καλός φίλος. Object: Βλέπω τον καλό φίλο. (The adjective drops the -ς just like the noun!) 2. Colors (Τα Χρώματα) Most colors are regular adjectives. They help you practice agreement every day. Κόκκινος, κόκκινη, κόκκινο (Red) Μαύρος, μαύρη, μαύρο (Black) Πράσινος, πράσινη, πράσινο (Green) TIP Foreign Colors: Some colors borrowed from other languages do NOT change. Μπλε (Blue), Ροζ (Pink), and Καφέ (Brown) stay the same regardless of gender! Ο μπλε ουρανός, η μπλε θάλασσα, το μπλε αυτοκίνητο. 3. Numbers 1-10 (Αριθμοί 1-10) Learning to count is the key to daily survival. Ένας / Μία / Ένα (One) Δύο (Two) Τρία (Three) Τέσσερα (Four) Πέντε (Five) Έξι (Six) Επτά (Seven) Οκτώ (Eight) Εννέα (Nine) Δέκα (Ten) IMPORTANT The number 1 is a chameleon! Good news: the number "One" is the exact same word as the indefinite articles (a/an) you learned in Unit 2! It must match the gender of what you are counting: Ένας άντρας (One man) Μία γυναίκα (One woman) Ένα παιδί (One child) Fun Fact: The numbers 3 and 4 are also mini-chameleons! You will learn their masculine/feminine forms later, but for now, use Τρία and Τέσσερα when counting out loud. 🎯 Unit 6 Mastery Checklist I can change the ending of καλός to match any noun gender. I know how to count to 10 in Greek. I remember that μπλε and ροζ never change their endings. Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Colors & Numbers Up Next: Unit 7: Asking Questions

adverbs.title: Unit 17: Adverbs & Expressions of Manner
adverbs.content: Unit 17: Adverbs & Expressions of Manner Adverbs are words that describe how , where , when , or how much an action happens. In Greek, adverbs are "frozen"—they never change their shape, which makes them very easy to learn. 1. Manner: How? These describe the "vibe" of an action. Σιγά (Slowly / Quietly) Γρήγορα (Quickly) Καλά (Well) Μαζί (Together) Έτσι (Like this / So) 2. Place: Where? Εδώ (Here) Εκεί (There) Πάνω (Up / On top) Κάτω (Down / Below) Μέσα (Inside) Έξω (Outside) 3. Time: When? Τώρα (Now) Σήμερα (Today) Αύριο (Tomorrow) Χθες (Yesterday) Πάντα (Always) Ποτέ (Never) CAUTION Spelling Alert: πότε vs ποτέ Be careful with the accent! Πότε; (Accent on first syllable) = When? Ποτέ (Accent on last syllable) = Never. 4. Quantity: How much? Πολύ (Very / Much) Λίγο (A little) Αρκετά (Enough / Quite) Καθόλου (Not at all / At all) 🎯 Unit 17 Mastery Checklist I can use the 10 most common adverbs correctly. I know the difference between Πότε (When?) and Ποτέ (Never). I understand that adverbs never change their endings to match nouns. Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Directions & Commands Up Next: Unit 18: Deponent Verbs (Passive Voice Intro)

ancient-remnants.title: Unit 31: Ancient Remnants & Formal Registers
ancient-remnants.content: Ancient Remnants in Modern Greek Welcome to Level C2: Mastery. Modern Greek is a living, breathing language, but it carries the ghosts of its 3,000-year history everywhere it goes. In the 19th and 20th centuries, Greece had two official languages: 1. Demotic (Δημοτική): The natural spoken language of the people. 2. Katharevousa (Καθαρεύουσα): An artificial, highly purified, "ancient-sounding" language used by the government, schools, and news anchors. In 1976, Demotic officially won and became the sole language of Greece. However, the 150 years of Katharevousa left deep scars on the modern language. To achieve true C2 fluency, you must understand the "Ancient Remnants" that are still used in the news, literature, and formal speech today. 1. Dative Expressions (The Lost Case) Ancient Greek had a 5th case: The Dative Case (used to show "to" or "for" someone). Modern Greek replaced this entirely by using "σε + Accusative". However, dozens of Dative phrases survived as frozen, highly formal expressions. You must memorize them as whole blocks: Εν τω μεταξύ (In the meantime) Δόξα τω Θεώ (Glory to God / Thank God) Εν πάση περιπτώσει (In any case / Anyway) Εν τέλει (In the end / Finally) Τοις μετρητοίς (In cash) NOTE The tiny word εν (in) is an ancient preposition that ALWAYS triggers the Dative case. You will see it constantly in C2 vocabulary! 2. Ancient Prepositions C2 Greek resurrects several ancient prepositions that demand the Genitive case . They are used in formal contexts, journalism, and academic writing. Ancient Preposition Meaning Modern Equivalent Example Ενώπιον (+ Gen) Before / In the presence of Μπροστά σε Ορκίστηκε ενώπιον του δικαστηρίου. Λόγω (+ Gen) Due to / Because of Επειδή / Εξαιτίας Κλειστό λόγω βροχής. Μέσω (+ Gen) Via / Through Από Έφτασα μέσω Αθηνών. Άνευ (+ Gen) Without Χωρίς Διασταύρωση άνευ σηματοδότη. Κατά (+ Gen) Against Εναντίον Διαδήλωση κατά της κυβέρνησης. 3. The 3rd Declension Nouns (Singular -εως / Plural -εις) Katharevousa dragged a group of ancient "3rd Declension" nouns into modern Greek. These are almost always abstract feminine nouns ending in -η (ancient -ις ). You will recognize them because they almost all end in -ση, -ξη, or -ψη ! Their defining feature is their formal Genitive singular ending: -εως . Η πόλη (The city) ➔ Το κέντρο της πόλεως (The center of the city). Η απόφαση (The decision) ➔ Η λήψη της αποφάσεως (The taking of the decision). IMPORTANT The Accent Shift Rule: If the word has more than two syllables (like κυβέρνηση ), the stress must move one syllable forward in the formal -εως genitive (➔ κυβερνήσεως ). If it has only two syllables (like πόλη ), the stress stays in the same place ( πόλεως ). The Plural (-εις): In the plural , these nouns ALWAYS use the ancient endings: Nominative/Accusative -εις and Genitive -εων . Οι αποφάσεις (The decisions) ➔ Των αποφάσεων . Οι πόλεις (The cities) ➔ Των πόλεων . 4. Bonus: C2 Adjectives & Participles To truly master formal Greek, you must recognize two ancient adjective classes that survive perfectly intact: The -ης / -ες Adjectives: These do not have separate forms for masculine and feminine. In the Genitive singular (for all genders), they take the ancient ending -ους . ακριβής (exact/precise), διεθνής (international), συνεχής (continuous). Genitive: Το αεροδρόμιο του διεθνούς δικτύου. The -ων / -ουσα / -ον Participles: These are ancient active participles that act like adjectives (similar to the English "-ing"). ενδιαφέρων (Masc), ενδιαφέρουσα (Fem), ενδιαφέρον (Neut) = Interesting. υπάρχων (existing), μέλλων (future). 🎯 Unit 31 Mastery Checklist I understand that Modern Greek contains formal remnants from Katharevousa and Ancient Greek. I have memorized common frozen Dative expressions like Εν τω μεταξύ . I know that words like Λόγω and Κατά are formal prepositions that take the Genitive case. I recognize the formal feminine Genitive ending -εως and the mandatory plural -εις / -εων . I can recognize learned C2 adjectives ending in -ης and participles like ενδιαφέρων . Practice Vocabulary: C2 Vocabulary: Ancient Phrases for Formal Greek Up Next: Unit 32: Genitive Absolute & Ancient Participles

aorist-past.title: Unit 14: The Past Tenses (Imperfect vs. Aorist)
aorist-past.content: Unit 14: The Past Tenses (Imperfect vs. Aorist) Welcome to the capstone of the A2 Expansion stage. You’ve learned how to build stems and how to talk about the future. Now, we look back. In Greek, just like in the future, we have two ways to talk about the past. TIP "Aorist" vs. "Simple Past" While many traditional textbooks and dictionaries use the ancient term "Aorist", modern grammarians prefer the term "Simple Past" , as it describes the contemporary language much more accurately. In this course, we will use both terms interchangeably. 1. The Two Past Tenses The Aorist (Simple Past/Perfective Past): Presents an action as having been completed at some specific point in the past (e.g., "I played"). Uses the Simple (Perfective) Stem. The Imperfect (Continuous Past/Imperfective Past): Presents an action, process, or state as occurring habitually or continuously in the past (e.g., "I was playing" or "I used to play"). Uses the Continuous (Present) Stem. 2. Past Tense Conjugation & The Augment Both past tenses (Imperfect and Aorist) use the exact same set of past-tense endings. Wait! The difference lies entirely in the Stem and the Augment. Pronoun Ending Example: γράφω (Simple Past) Εγώ -α έ-γραψ- α Εσύ -ες έ-γραψ- ες Αυτός/ή/ό -ε έ-γραψ- ε Εμείς -αμε γράψ- αμε Εσείς -ατε γράψ- ατε Αυτοί/ές/ά -αν(ε) έ-γραψ- αν / γράψ- ανε NOTE Colloquial Tip: The 3rd person plural form with the extra -ε ( γράψανε , διαβάζανε ) is extremely frequent in everyday spoken language, but less often used in formal writing. IMPORTANT The Augment (έ- and ή-) Past tense forms in Greek always carry their stress on the third syllable from the end . Syllabic Augment (έ-): If the verb stem plus the ending only has two syllables, Greek prefixes an έ- to the beginning to carry the stress. This is why it appears in short forms ( έ-γρα-ψα ) but completely drops off in longer forms where the stress naturally shifts to the right ( γρά-ψα-με , γρά-ψα-τε ). Vocalic Augment (ή-): Verbs starting with a vowel often change their initial vowel to ή- in the past (e.g., ελπίζω -> ή λπιζα). 3. Side-by-Side: Video vs. Photo Let's use the verb παίζω (I play). Continuous Stem: παίζ- Simple Stem: παίξ- A. The Imperfect (The Video) Έπαιζα τένις κάθε μέρα. (I used to play tennis every day). Use this for background info: "While I was playing , it started to rain." B. The Aorist (The Photo) Έπαιξα τένις χθες. (I played tennis yesterday—once). Use this for specific, completed events: "I went to the park." 4. The Absolute Irregulars These are the most common verbs in the past. You must memorize them. Notice how the first three have completely irregular 1-syllable stems, meaning they do not take the έ- augment in the Aorist (though they do in the Imperfect!). βλέπω (see) ➔ είδα (I saw) λέω (say) ➔ είπα (I said) πάω (go) ➔ πήγα (I went) θέλω (want) ➔ ήθελα (I wanted) - Uses Vocalic Augment! ξέρω (know) ➔ ήξερα (I knew) - Uses Vocalic Augment! CAUTION No "Photo" for Being & Having! The verbs έχω (have) and είμαι (be) are what grammarians call Defective Verbs . This means they only exist in the Continuous (Imperfective) aspect. έχω ➔ είχα (I had) είμαι ➔ ήμουν(α) (I was) They do not have an Aorist (Photo) form. You cannot say "I was" or "I had" as a single, completed dot in time in Greek. 🎯 A2 Expansion Capstone Congratulations! You have completed the A2 Stage . You have mastered: Pluralization of all nouns and adjectives. The "Big Two" Verb Groups (B1 & B2). Verbal Aspect (Video vs. Photo) and the Subjunctive ( να ). Both Future tenses and the mechanics of the Simple Stem. Both Past tenses (Imperfect vs. Aorist) and the Augment rules. Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Time & Calendar Up Next: Unit 15: The Genitive Case (Possession)

articles.title: articles
articles.content: The Articles (Τα Άρθρα) In Greek, almost every noun must be preceded by an article (the little words for "the" or "a/an"). You cannot ignore them. If you drop the article in a Greek sentence, you will sound like a caveman ("Me go store. Buy apple.") The article is the anchor of the sentence. It tells the listener the gender (male/female/neuter) and the number (singular/plural) of the noun that is about to follow. 1. The Definite Article ("The") We use the definite article when we are talking about a specific thing (e.g., "The car is red"). In English, we only have one word for this: The . In Greek, "The" shape-shifts based on the gender of the noun. You already learned these in the previous lesson! Gender "The" (Singular) Example Translation Masculine ο ο δρόμος (o dró-mos) the street Feminine η η γυναίκα (ee yi-né-ka) the woman Neuter το το παιδί (to pe-dí) the child CAUTION Names Take Articles! In English, we never put "The" in front of someone's name. You don't say "The Maria is here." In Greek, you absolutely must. Ο Γιώργος είναι εδώ. (George is here). Η Μαρία είναι εδώ. (Maria is here). 2. The Indefinite Article ("A / An") We use the indefinite article when we are talking about a non-specific thing (e.g., "I want a car"). Just like "The", the Greek word for "A" changes based on the gender. Gender "A / An" Example Translation Masculine ένας ένας δρόμος (é-nas dró-mos) a street Feminine μία / μια μια γυναίκα (mya yi-né-ka) a woman Neuter ένα ένα παιδί (é-na pe-dí) a child Notice how the indefinite articles share the same endings as the nouns they describe? ένας ends in -ας (masculine), μία ends in -α (feminine), and ένα ends in -α (neuter rule-breaker, but very easy to remember). IMPORTANT The Object Form (Accusative) Just like "The" ( ο ➔ τον ), the masculine "A" drops its "s" when it receives the action: ένας ➔ έναν . Έχω έναν σκύλο (I have a dog). Good news: The feminine ( μία/μια ) and neuter ( ένα ) forms never change ! Έχω μια γάτα (I have a cat). TIP μία vs. μια You will see the feminine article spelled both ways. Μία (mí-a) has two distinct syllables. It is slightly more formal and is also used as the number "1". Μια (mya) is a single syllable. It is used in fast, casual speech. They mean exactly the same thing. Example Sentences Let's look at how replacing "The" with "A" changes the meaning exactly as it does in English. Ο φίλος είναι εδώ. ➔ The (specific) friend is here. Ένας φίλος είναι εδώ. ➔ A (random) friend is here. Θέλω το βιβλίο. ➔ I want the (specific) book. Θέλω ένα βιβλίο. ➔ I want a (random) book. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist I will literally never say a Greek noun or a person's name without putting "The" ( ο, η, το ) or "A" ( ένας, μία, ένα ) in front of it. I know that "μία" and "μια" are the exact same word.

basics.title: Unit 1: The Greek Writing System Masterclass
basics.content: Unit 1: The Greek Writing System Masterclass Welcome to your first step toward mastering Greek! As an English speaker, learning the Greek alphabet is much easier than you might think. Over 25% of English vocabulary has Greek roots—you already know more Greek than you realize. This comprehensive guide covers everything you need to read and write Modern Greek: Single Letters, Digraphs (Sound Combinations), Accents, and Punctuation. 1. The 24 Letters The Greek alphabet consists of 24 letters. To make them easier to learn, we can group them into three categories: Team A: The "Freebies" These look and sound almost exactly like their English counterparts. Letter Name Sound Example Α α Alpha [a] a pple Ε ε Epsilon [e] p e t Ι ι Iota [ee] m ee t Κ κ Kappa [k] k ite Μ μ Mu [m] m om Ν ν Nu [n] n o Ο ο Omicron [o] t o p Τ τ Tau [t] t op Ζ ζ Zeta [z] z oo TIP Pro-Tip for English Speakers: The voiceless consonants π [p], τ [t], and κ [k] are pronounced without "aspiration" . Unlike in English, where you release a puff of air when saying words like pot or cat, in Greek these letters have no expulsion of breath, sounding more like the crisp 'p' in spot or 't' in stay. Team B: The "False Friends" These look like English letters but represent different sounds. Letter Name Sound Example Β β Beta [v] v an (not 'B') Η η Eta [ee] m ee t (not 'H') Ρ ρ Rho [r] r olled R (not 'P') Χ χ Chi [h] Scottish ch (loch) Υ υ Upsilon [ee] m ee t (not 'Y' or 'U') Team C: The "Aliens" These shapes are unique to the Greek alphabet. Letter Name Sound Example Γ γ Gamma [j] or [g] y es (before e/i) Δ δ Delta [th] th is (soft) Θ θ Theta [th] th ink (hard) Λ λ Lambda [l] l ion Ξ ξ Xi [ks] bo x Π π Pi [p] p en Σ σ, ς Sigma [s] or [z] s un (z before β, γ, δ) Φ φ Phi [f] f ish Ψ ψ Psi [ps] o ps Ω ω Omega [o] t o p 2. Digraphs (Letter Combinations) Sometimes Greek glues two letters together to create a single new sound. Vowel Twins αι = [e] (αιτία - cause) ει, οι, υι = [ee] (είμαι - I am) ου = [oo] (ουρανός - sky) TIP The Tricky Vowel Twins (αυ & ευ): When α or ε are combined with υ , the υ transforms into a consonant sound! αυ / ευ sound like [af] / [ef] before voiceless consonants. αυ / ευ sound like [av] / [ev] before vowels or voiced consonants. Consonant Twins (The Hard Sounds) Over centuries, some original sounds softened. Modern Greek uses these combinations to regain those "hard" sounds. μπ = B (μπαμπάς - dad) ντ = D (ντύνω - to dress) γκ / γγ = G (άγκυρα - anchor) τσ = TS (τσέπη - pocket) τζ = J / DZ (τζάκι - fireplace) 3. Accents & Punctuation Greek is highly phonetic, and its punctuation marks are unique. The Tonos (´) Every word with 2+ syllables has an accent mark to show the stressed syllable. νερό (ne-RÓ) - water πρόβλημα (PRÓ-vli-ma) - problem Punctuation Marks The Question Mark (;): The Greek question mark is the English semicolon. The Upper Dot (·): Used like a colon or semicolon for a medium break. Quotation Marks (« »): Used for dialogue and quotes. 🎯 Unit 1 Mastery Checklist I can recognize all 24 letters and their sounds. I know the difference between "False Friends" and "Aliens." I identify the [ee] sound in its various forms (ι, η, υ, ει, οι, υι). I understand how to pronounce αυ and ευ in context. I can use the Tonos (´) to correctly stress a word. I recognize the Greek question mark ( ; ). Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Survival Phrases Up Next: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns & Articles

compound-words.title: Unit 30: Compound Words & Prefixation
compound-words.content: Unit 30: Compound Words & Prefixation Greek vocabulary is massive, but it's incredibly logical. Throughout its long history, Greek has always had a great facility for creating new words. Instead of inventing brand new words for every concept, Greeks build words like Lego bricks by modifying stems with prefixes, or snapping two smaller words together. Once you understand how the bricks connect, you can usually guess the meaning of a C1/C2 word even if you've never seen it before! 1. The Greek "Glue": The letter Omikron (o) When you attach two Greek words together to form a compound, you almost always need "glue" to connect them. The elements that make up a compound are typically linked by the vowel -ο- . If the first word ends in a normal vowel (like -ος, -η, or -ι), that vowel simply turns into an ο . Then the second word attaches to it! βιβλίο (book) + πωλώ (I sell) ➔ βιβλιοπωλείο (bookstore). μαχαίρι (knife) + πιρούνι (fork) ➔ μαχαιροπίρουνο (knife and fork). Σάββατο (Saturday) + Κυριακή (Sunday) ➔ Σαββατοκύριακο (weekend). 2. Prefixes (The Power Bricks) There are dozens of highly active prefixes (mini-words) that you can slap onto the front of any word to instantly change its meaning. Α- / Αν- (The Negative / Un-) This is the famous Alpha Privative. Greek uses a simple α- (if the next letter is a consonant) or αν- (if the next letter is a vowel). IMPORTANT C1 Pronunciation Secret: When adding the alpha privative to form a negative adjective, the stress normally shifts to the third syllable from the end ! γνωστός (known) ➔ άγνωστος (unknown). φυσικός (natural) ➔ αφύσικος (unnatural). πιθανός (probable) ➔ απίθανος (improbable / amazing). Ξε- (The Reverser / Un-) Prefixed to verbs to reverse the action (like tying and un-tying). κλειδώνω (I lock) ➔ ξεκλειδώνω (I unlock). ντύνω (I dress) ➔ ξεντύνω (I undress). διψώ (I am thirsty) ➔ ξεδιψώ (I quench my thirst). Ξανα- (Again / Re-) If you want to say "do it again" or "re-do", just add ξανα- to the verb! κάνω (I do) ➔ ξανακάνω (I re-do). διαβάζω (I read) ➔ ξαναδιαβάζω (I reread). TIP Bonus C1 Prefix: Κατα- (Very much) You can stick κατα- in front of adjectives to mean "extremely", or verbs to intensify the action. άσπρος (white) ➔ κάτασπρος (very white). βρέχω (I wet) ➔ καταβρέχω (I soak / drench). 3. Advanced Combinations You can stick almost any parts of speech together to create powerful descriptive words. Always remember to use the glue ο ! Adjective + Adjective: ασπρόμαυρος (black and white). Adjective + Noun: κοκκινομάλλης (red-haired), στενόμυαλος (narrow-minded). Adjective/Adverb + Participle: φρεσκοβαμμένος (freshly painted), μαυροντυμένος (dressed in black). 🎭 The Action Combos (Verb + Verb) Greek frequently creates compounds by sticking two verbs together, usually connected with the ο glue. ανοίγω (open) + κλείνω (close) ➔ ανοιγοκλείνω (I open and close). πηγαίνω (go) + έρχομαι (come) ➔ πηγαινοέρχομαι (I come and go / I pacing). ανεβαίνω (go up) + κατεβαίνω (go down) ➔ ανεβοκατεβαίνω (I go up and down). 4. Famous Greek Roots Once you hit C1, you will see these roots everywhere. -φίλος (Lover / Friend): ζωόφιλος (animal lover), φιλόξενος (hospitable). -φόβος (Fearer / Hater): ξενοφοβία (fear of foreigners), υψοφοβία (fear of heights). -λόγος (Speaker / Student of): ψυχολόγος (psychologist). -γράφος (Writer): μυθιστοριογράφος (novelist). 🎯 Unit 30 Mastery Checklist I know that Greek builds words using the letter ο as "glue". I understand the stress shift rule for the Alpha Privative ( α- / αν- ). I can reverse an action using ξε- and repeat it using ξανα- . I can identify and use advanced combinations like ανοιγοκλείνω . Practice Vocabulary: C1 Vocabulary: Compound Words in Everyday Life Up Next: Unit 31: Ancient Remnants & Formal Registers

conditionals-1-2.title: Unit 21: Conditionals (The 'If' Clauses)
conditionals-1-2.content: Unit 21: Conditionals (The 'If' Clauses) Conditional sentences are "If X, then Y" statements. In Greek grammar, the "If" clause is referred to as the protasis , and the main "then" clause is the apodosis . The standard word for "If" is Αν . Depending on how likely the situation is to happen, conditional constructions are divided into two main categories: Factual (Real) and Counterfactual (Unreal). TIP Native Alternatives to "Αν" While Αν is the most common word, you will often hear Greeks use άμα (if / whenever), the more formal εάν (if), or εφόσον (provided that) to introduce an "if" clause. 1. Type 1: The Factual (Real) Use this for facts, plans, or likely events. If the condition is fulfilled, the main clause happens. Formula: Αν + [Any Tense except Pluperfect], then [Any Tense/Command]. Even though you can use almost any tense, the most common combination is Present/Future in the "If" clause, and Future/Imperative in the main clause. Αν προσπαθείς να τον πείσεις, κάνεις μεγάλο λάθος. (If you are trying to persuade him, you are making a big mistake). Άμα σε ενοχλεί ο καπνός, άνοιξε το παράθυρο. (If the smoke bothers you, open the window). Αν τελειώσετε νωρίς, ελάτε να μας δείτε. (If you finish early, come to see us). WARNING Negating the "If" How do you say "If you don't..."? Even though "If" phrases feel hypothetical, conditional clauses introduced by αν or άμα are strictly negated by the indicative particle δεν (NOT μην). Αν δεν βρέχει... (If it does not rain...). 2. Type 2: The Unreal (Dreaming / Present Counterfactual) Use this for hypotheticals, dreams, or giving advice that are not being realized in the present. Greek pushes both verbs into the Continuous Past (Imperfect) . Formula: Αν + [Imperfect], then Θα + [Imperfect] (The Conditional). Αν είχα λεφτά, θα αγόραζα ένα πλοίο. (If I had money [now], I would buy a boat). Αν καταλάβαινες τη στενοχώρια μου, δεν θα μιλούσες έτσι. (If you understood my distress, you wouldn't speak like this). 3. Type 3: The Regret (Past Counterfactual) Use this for things that never happened in the past. It’s too late! Formula: Αν + [Pluperfect], then Θα + [Pluperfect] (The Perfect Conditional). Αν είχε στείλει εκείνο το γράμμα, θα τον είχε πείσει . (If she had sent that letter, she would have persuaded him). Δεν θα τον παντρευότανε ποτέ, αν δεν τον αγαπούσε . (She would never have married him, if she didn't love him). TIP The Imperfect Secret! Greek is incredibly flexible here. When referring to the past, you don't have to use the long Pluperfect tenses. You can actually just use the Imperfect tense (Type 2) to talk about the past!. Αν έστελνε εκείνο το γράμμα, θα τον έπειθε . Depending on the context, this can mean either "If she sent that letter [now], she would persuade him" OR "If she had sent that letter [yesterday], she would have persuaded him". 🎯 Unit 21 Mastery Checklist I know that Αν, άμα, and εάν mean "If." I understand the difference between the protasis (the "if" clause) and the apodosis (the "then" clause). I remember to use δεν (not μην ) to negate an Αν clause. I understand that for "Would," Greek uses Θα + Past Tense (The Conditional). I can build a hypothetical dream sentence using the Imperfect tense, and I know it can be used for past regrets too! Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Travel & Life Experiences Up Next: Unit 22: Advanced Particles & Interjections (Spoken Expressions)

deponent-verbs.title: Unit 18: Passive Voice & Deponent Verbs (-ομαι)
deponent-verbs.content: Unit 18: Passive Voice & Deponent Verbs (-ομαι) Welcome to Type C verbs. These verbs don’t end in -ω ; they end in -ομαι (o-me). This ending system is used for two very different things in Greek: the Passive Voice and Deponent Verbs. 1. What are Deponent Verbs? Some of the most common verbs in Greek look passive but are actually active in meaning. These are called Deponent Verbs . They only exist in the -ομαι form. έρχομαι (I come) ➔ Από πού έρχεσαι; αισθάνομαι (I feel) ➔ Αισθάνομαι καλά. χρειάζομαι (I need) ➔ Χρειάζομαι βοήθεια. σκέφτομαι (I think) ➔ Τι σκέφτεσαι; φοβάμαι / ντρέπομαι (I fear / I am ashamed) CAUTION The "Semi-Deponents" Some very common verbs are "semi-deponent". This means they look passive in the Present and Imperfect tenses, but they suddenly switch to Active endings in the Simple Past! γίνομαι (I become) ➔ Past: έγινα (I became). έρχομαι (I come) ➔ Past: ήρθα (I came). κάθομαι (I sit) ➔ Past: κάθισα (I sat). 2. Present Tense Conjugation Both Passive and Deponent verbs of the First Conjugation use these "Type C" endings: Pronoun Ending Example: έρχομαι (I come) (Εγώ) -ομαι έρχ ομαι (Εσύ) -εσαι έρχ εσαι (Αυτό/ή/ό) -εται έρχ εται (Εμείς) -όμαστε ερχ όμαστε (Εσείς) -εστε / -όσαστε έρχ εστε / ερχ όσαστε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ονται έρχ ονται TIP The Accent Shift & Colloquial Forms Notice that in the "We" form ( ερχόμαστε ), the accent mark jumps one syllable to the right. This is a very common rhythmic rule in Greek! Also, for the "You all" form, -εστε is standard, but -όσαστε (with the same accent shift) is a very frequent colloquial alternative. 3. The True Passive Voice You can also take an active verb (like πλένω - I wash) and flip it to the passive form ( πλένομαι - I am washed). The passive voice is often used reflexively in Greek (to do something to oneself). Active: Πλένω το αυτοκίνητο. (I wash the car). Passive/Reflexive: Πλένομαι . (I am washing myself / I am taking a bath). "By Someone" (Ο Ποιητής / The Agent) To say "by" in a passive sentence, you use the preposition από + Accusative. The object of the active sentence becomes the subject of the passive one. Active: Ο Γιώργος διαβάζει το βιβλίο. (George reads the book). Passive: Το βιβλίο διαβάζεται από τον Γιώργο. (The book is read by George). 4. Past Passive (Aorist) The simple past tense of passive verbs is characterized by the extra syllable -ηκ- (or variations of it) placed right before the past endings. The stress always falls on the third syllable from the end. Pronoun Ending Example: πλένομαι ➔ πλύθηκα (Εγώ) -ηκα πλύθηκα (I was washed) (Εσύ) -ηκες πλύθηκες (Αυτός/ή/ό) -ηκε πλύθηκε (Εμείς) -ήκαμε πλυθήκαμε (Εσείς) -ήκατε πλυθήκατε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ηκαν / -ήκανε πλύθηκαν / πλυθήκανε WARNING The Consonant "Cheat Codes" Return! Do you remember the simple stem groups (Σ, Ξ, Ψ) from Unit 12? The consonant before the -ηκα ending changes depending on the active stem! The 'S' Group (ζω, νω, θω): Uses -στηκα or -θηκα . ετοιμάζω ➔ ετοιμά στ ηκα (I got ready). δένω ➔ δέ θ ηκα (I was tied). The 'PS' (Lip) Group (πω, βω, φω, -εύω): Uses -φτηκα (or formally -φθηκα ). κρύβω ➔ κρύ φτ ηκα (I hid myself). The 'X' (Throat) Group (κω, γω, χω): Uses -χτηκα (or formally -χθηκα ). ανοίγω ➔ ανοί χτ ηκα (I was opened). 🎯 Unit 18 Mastery Checklist I can conjugate έρχομαι in the present tense, remembering the accent shift in ερχόμαστε . I know that verbs ending in -ομαι can be either Active (Deponent) or Passive/Reflexive. I know what a "semi-deponent" verb is ( έρχομαι ➔ ήρθα ). I can use από + Accusative to express "by someone" in a passive sentence. I recognize the -ηκα, -στηκα, -φτηκα, -χτηκα endings as markers of the Past Passive. Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Deponent Verbs & States Up Next: Unit 19: Voices & Moods Review

diminutives.title: Unit 29: Diminutives & Augmentatives (Υποκοριστικά & Μεγεθυντικά)
diminutives.content: Diminutives & Augmentatives (Υποκοριστικά & Μεγεθυντικά) Greek is a language of intense emotion. You don't just drink a coffee; you drink a little coffee with a friend. You don't just see a dog; you see a huge, monstrous dog. To express these feelings of smallness, affection, or enormous size, Greek uses special suffixes attached to the stem of nouns. Mastering these suffixes is the key to sounding like a true native speaker! 1. The Diminutives (Making things small, cute, or polite) Diminutives express small size, familiarity, affection, or sometimes even depreciation. They are incredibly common in spoken Greek. When you order food, you rarely order a "τυρόπιτα" (cheese pie). You order a "τυροπιτάκι" (little cheese pie), because it sounds friendlier! Suffix 1: -άκι (The Universal Tiny-Maker) This is by far the most common diminutive. It can be added to almost any noun, and it always turns the word into a Neuter noun (Το). το σπίτι (the house) ➔ το σπιτάκι (the little house). το παιδί (the child) ➔ το παιδάκι (the small child). ο καφές (the coffee) ➔ το καφεδάκι (the small coffee). ο μεζές (the appetizer) ➔ το μεζεδάκι (the snack). (Notice how even masculine nouns become neuter when you add -άκι!) Suffix 2: -ίτσα & -ούλα (For Feminine Nouns) If you want to keep a word feminine while making it cute, you can use these suffixes attached to the stem. η μπύρα (the beer) ➔ η μπιρίτσα (the little beer). η ώρα (the hour) ➔ η ωρίτσα (a brief hour or so). η αδερφή (the sister) ➔ η αδερφούλα (the little sister). η βάρκα (the boat) ➔ η βαρκούλα (the little boat). (These are also very common for female names: η Ελένη ➔ η Ελενίτσα ). Suffix 3: -ούλης & -άκης (For Masculine Nouns) If you want to make a masculine person sound cute or endearing. ο πατέρας (the father) ➔ ο πατερούλης (daddy). ο κόσμος (the world/people) ➔ ο κοσμάκης (the ordinary people). (The -άκης suffix is also extremely common for male proper names, like Μανωλάκης). TIP C1 Advanced Tip: Diminutive Adjectives! You can also make adjectives cute or "moderate"! Greek uses the suffix -ούτσικος to mean "to a moderate extent" (like the English "-ish"). καλός (good) ➔ καλούτσικος (quite good). μεγάλος (big) ➔ μεγαλούτσικος (biggish). ξινό (sour) ➔ ξινούτσικο (a little sour). 2. The Augmentatives (Making things HUGE) Sometimes you need to emphasize that something is massive, impressive, or even a bit scary. Augmentatives express large size or admiration. Suffix 1: -αρος (The Giant) This is the most common masculine augmentative. το σκυλί (the dog) ➔ ο σκύλαρος (the great big dog). το παιδί (the boy/lad) ➔ ο παίδαρος (the big, handsome lad). Suffix 2: -άρα & -α (The Huge Feminine) This creates a sense of enormous, sometimes overwhelming size. το αυτοκίνητο (the car) ➔ η αυτοκινητάρα (the huge, impressive car). η κοιλιά (the belly) ➔ η κοιλάρα (the big belly). το μπουκάλι (the bottle) ➔ η μπουκάλα (the big bottle). IMPORTANT Native Secret: The "Possessor of a Quality" (-αράς / -ακλάς) If you want to describe a man who has a HUGE amount of a certain quality, use -αράς or -ακλάς . η δουλειά (work) ➔ ο δουλευταράς (the incredibly hard worker). η φωνή (voice) ➔ ο φωνακλάς (the loud-mouth). 3. The Pragmatic Power of Emotion Remember, these suffixes aren't just about physical size. Greek often uses the diminutive forms of nouns and adjectives with acts like offers, compliments, and requests as a device to show affection or to minimize the imposition placed by the speaker on the addressee. If a friend invites you to their house and says, "Πάρτε ένα μπισκοτάκι" (Take a little biscuit), they are using the diminutive to make the offer sound polite and unobtrusive. When you ask a waiter, "Μας φέρνετε μια μπιρίτσα;" (Can you bring us a little beer?), it sounds incredibly friendly and casual. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist I know that -άκι makes words small/cute, and always turns them into Το (Neuter). I know the feminine diminutives -ίτσα and -ούλα . I know the masculine diminutives -ούλης and -άκης . I can use -ούτσικος to say an adjective is "moderate" or "-ish". I know that -αρος, -άρα, and -αράς are used to make things sound completely massive! Practice Vocabulary: C1 Vocabulary: Nuance & Emotion Up Next: Unit 30: Compound Words

exercise-actions.title: Unit 4-5: Accusative Action
exercise-actions.content: Unit 4-5: Action & The Accusative This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (A rainy street in Thessaloniki, Greece. People holding umbrellas and hurrying) Power Lexis: Greek Pronunciation English Η βροχή (i vro-chí) The rain Η ομπρέλα (i om-bré-la) The umbrella Κρατάω (kra-táo) I hold Τρέχω (tré-kho) I run Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Use one Type A action verb (e.g. κρατάω, τρέχω). 2. Include a direct object in the Accusative case . Pro Tip: Remember that feminine nouns keep the "α" or "η" in the accusative, but the definite article changes from "η" to "την". 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Βρέχει πολύ σήμερα. Ο άνθρωπος κρατάει την ομπρέλα.» (It is raining a lot today. The man is holding the umbrella.) Why it works: κρατάει την ομπρέλα : "Κρατάει" is the 3rd person singular of the Type A action verb "κρατάω". "Την ομπρέλα" is the direct object receiving the action, correctly inflected in the accusative case (changing from "Η ομπρέλα" to "Την ομπρέλα"). 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base Sentence Trigger Target Sentence Διαβάζω το βιβλίο. (I read the book.) Change subject to Ο δάσκαλος Ο δάσκαλος διαβάζει το βιβλίο. Κρατάει την ομπρέλα. (He/She holds the umbrella.) Change subject to Εγώ (I) Κρατάω την ομπρέλα. Τρέχω γρήγορα. (I run fast.) Change subject to Η γυναίκα (The woman) Η γυναίκα τρέχει γρήγορα. Βλέπει τον καφέ. (He/She sees the coffee.) Change subject to Εμείς (We) Βλέπουμε τον καφέ. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of the Accusative Case and Type A verbs. «Βλέπω την τσάντα. Η γυναίκα κρατάει την τσάντα. Βλέπω τον καφέ. Ο άντρας πίνει τον καφέ. Εγώ έχω το νερό. Πίνω το νερό και βλέπω τον ουρανό. Κάνω μία βόλτα.»

exercise-adjectives.title: Unit 6-7: Adjectives & Objects
exercise-adjectives.content: Unit 6-7: Adjectives & Questions This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (Two people looking at colorful fresh fruit at an outdoor Greek market under the sunlight) Power Lexis: Greek Pronunciation English Φρέσκος, -η, -ο (frés-kos) Fresh Κόκκινος, -η, -ο (kó-ki-nos) Red Πόσο (pó-so) How much Κάνει (ká-ni) It costs (literally "it makes") Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Ask a question using the essential phrase Πόσο κάνει; 2. Use an adjective agreeing in gender with its noun. Pro Tip: In Greek, adjectives must always match the gender (masculine, feminine, or neuter), number, and case of the noun they describe. 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Αυτό το φρούτο είναι κόκκινο και φρέσκο. Πόσο κάνει;» (This fruit is red and fresh. How much does it cost?) Why it works: κόκκινο και φρέσκο : The noun "το φρούτο" is neuter. Therefore, the adjectives "κόκκινος" and "φρέσκος" must drop their masculine "-ος" ending and take the neuter "-ο" ending to become "κόκκινο" and "φρέσκο". Πόσο κάνει; : This is the standard, everyday way to ask for the price of a singular item in Greece. 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base Sentence Trigger Target Sentence Το κόκκινο μήλο κάνει δύο ευρώ. (The red apple costs two euros.) Change subject to Ο καφές (Masculine) Ο κόκκινος καφές κάνει δύο ευρώ. Πόσο κάνει η μικρή τσάντα; (How much does the small bag cost?) Change subject to Το βιβλίο (Neuter) Πόσο κάνει το μικρό βιβλίο; Το φρέσκο νερό είναι κρύο. (The fresh water is cold.) Change subject to Η σαλάτα (Feminine) Η φρέσκια σαλάτα είναι κρύα. Έχω ένα μεγάλο πρόβλημα. (I have a big problem - Neuter) Change object to Η ιδέα (Feminine) Έχω μία μεγάλη ιδέα. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of adjectives and basic questions. «Το μεγάλο σπίτι έχει μία κόκκινη πόρτα. Η μικρή πόρτα είναι ανοιχτή. Ποιος είναι εκεί; Ένας καλός φίλος είναι εκεί. Τι θέλει; Θέλει λίγο κρύο νερό. Πόσο κάνει το κρύο νερό; Είναι δωρεάν!»

exercise-basics.title: Unit 1-3: The Basics
exercise-basics.content: Unit 1-3: Foundations This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (An old Greek grandmother cooking in a rustic, sunlight-filled kitchen) Power Lexis: Greek Pronunciation English Η γιαγιά (i ya-yá) The grandmother Το φαγητό (to fa-yi-tó) The food Είναι (í-ne) He/She/It is Έχω (é-kho) I have Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Describe the scene using the verb "to be" (είμαι). 2. Describe an action/possession using the verb "to have" (έχω). Pro Tip: Keep it simple! Focus on creating short, complete sentences that use the definite article correctly. 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Η γιαγιά είναι στην κουζίνα. Έχει ωραίο φαγητό.» (The grandmother is in the kitchen. She has nice food.) Why it works: Η γιαγιά είναι... : Correct use of the feminine definite article "η" with the subject noun and the 3rd person singular present tense form of "είμαι". Έχει ωραίο φαγητό : The verb "έχω" is conjugated to the 3rd person singular ("έχει" - she has). "Ωραίο φαγητό" (nice food) follows as the related object of possession. 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base Sentence Trigger Target Sentence Εγώ είμαι στην κουζίνα. (I am in the kitchen.) Change subject to Η γιαγιά Η γιαγιά είναι στην κουζίνα. Έχω ωραίο φαγητό. (I have nice food.) Change subject to Ο πατέρας Ο πατέρας έχει ωραίο φαγητό. Ο φίλος είναι εδώ. (The friend is here.) Change subject to Εμείς (We) Εμείς είμαστε εδώ. Η μητέρα έχει ένα βιβλίο. (The mother has a book.) Change subject to Εσύ (You) Εσύ έχεις ένα βιβλίο. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of to be and to have . «Το σπίτι είναι μεγάλο. Η γιαγιά είναι στην κουζίνα. Έχει ωραίο φαγητό. Το παιδί είναι εδώ. Έχει ένα βιβλίο. Το βιβλίο είναι καλό. Η μητέρα είναι και αυτή εδώ. Είναι όλοι καλά!»

exercise-plurals.title: Unit 8-10: Plurals & Verbs
exercise-plurals.content: Unit 8-10: Plurals & Verb Groups This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (A stylish woman trying on different coats in a high-end boutique in Athens) Power Lexis: Greek Pronunciation English Το παλτό -> Τα παλτά (to pal-tó) -> (ta pal-tá) The coat Μικρός, -ή, -ό -> Μικρά (mi-krós) -> (mi-krá) Small Φοράω (fo-ráo) I wear Ακριβός, -ή, -ό (a-kri-vós) Expensive Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Use Plural Nouns and Adjectives . 2. Conjgate a Group B verb (e.g. φοράω). Pro Tip: Neuter nouns ending in -ο or stressed -ό in the singular change to -α or stressed -ά in the plural. Adjectives follow the exact same pattern! 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Η γυναίκα φοράει τα μικρά παλτά. Είναι πολύ ακριβά!» (The woman is wearing the small coats. They are very expensive!) Why it works: τα μικρά παλτά / πολύ ακριβά : "Το παλτό" becomes "Τα παλτά" in the plural. Accordingly, the adjectives "μικρό" and "ακριβό" change their endings to match the neuter plural form (μικρά, ακριβά). φοράει : "Φοράω" belongs to Verb Group B1 (verbs ending in an unstressed -άω in the "I" form). The "she" form takes the -άει ending. 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base Sentence Trigger Target Sentence Η γυναίκα φοράει το παλτό. (The woman wears the coat.) Change object to Plural Η γυναίκα φοράει τα παλτά. Το μικρό παιδί παίζει. (The small child plays.) Change subject to Plural Τα μικρά παιδιά παίζουν. Αγοράζω το ακριβό ρούχο. (I buy the expensive piece of clothing.) Change object to Plural Αγοράζω τα ακριβά ρούχα. Ο πωλητής μιλάει πολύ. (The salesperson talks a lot.) Change subject to Plural Οι πωλητές μιλούν πολύ. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of Plural Nouns and Group B Verbs. «Τα μαγαζιά είναι ακριβά. Οι γυναίκες αγαπούν τα ωραία ρούχα. Δοκιμάζουν τα μικρά φορέματα και τα μεγάλα παλτά. Οι πωλητές μιλούν πολύ. Τα ρούχα κοστίζουν πολλά ευρώ. Όλοι γελάνε.»

future-continuous.title: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα) & Aspect
future-continuous.content: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα) & Aspect In Unit 10, you learned the magic of Aspect. Now, we apply that "Video vs. Photo" logic to the future. In Greek, we don't just say "I will play"; we specify if it's an ongoing habit or a one-time completed thing. 1. The Future Particle: Θα Just like να and ας , the word θα is a particle that sits right in front of the verb to express actions expected to take place in the future. It translates to the English "will". English: I will play. Greek: Θα [verb]. IMPORTANT The Pronoun Sandwich The particle θα can only be separated from the verb by weak object pronouns. If you have weak pronouns (like "me", "it", "him"), they must sneak between θα and the verb. Θα το κάνω. (I will do it). Θα σου το δώσει. (He will give it to you). 2. Continuous (Imperfective) Future: The Video If you want to talk about a habit, a routine, or something you will be doing repeatedly or continuously in the future, use the Continuous (Imperfective) Future . Formula: Θα + Present Tense Verb Θα διαβάζω κάθε μέρα. (I will [be reading] every day). Κάθε Τετάρτη θα τρώμε στην ταβέρνα. (Every Wednesday we will eat at the taverna). 3. Simple (Perfective) Future: The Photo If you are talking about a single, specific event that will happen and be completed at a particular point in time, use the Simple (Perfective) Future . Formula: Θα + Dependent Form (Perfective Non-Past Stem + Present Endings) Θα διαβάσω το βιβλίο αύριο. (I will read the book tomorrow—once). Θα συναντήσω τη Μαίρη αύριο. (I will meet Mary tomorrow). CAUTION The Augment Trap (No Past Markings!) Even though the Simple Future relies on the perfective stem (often found by looking at the Simple Past tense), you must NEVER add the past tense augment (the prefix ε- ) to the future. The augment is strictly a past tense marker! Simple Past: έ γραψα (I wrote). Simple Future: θα γράψω (I will write - Notice the 'ε' is gone! ). 4. The Negation Combo To negate a future sentence ("I will not" / "I won't"), you must use the indicative negative particle δεν . It is always placed immediately before θα . Δεν θα παίζω (I won't be playing regularly). Δεν θα παίξω (I won't play this specific time). Δεν θα σου το δώσει (He won't give it to you). TIP Bonus Native Secret: "Θα" for Probability Greeks don't just use θα for the future. They also use it to express probability or supposition, similar to the English "must" or "must have"! Θα έφτασε. (He must have arrived - using the Simple Past ). Θα είναι σπίτι. (He must be at home - using the Present ). 🎯 Unit 11 Mastery Checklist I can choose between θα παίζω (Continuous/habit) and θα παίξω (Simple/one-off). I know how to negate the future with δεν θα . I remember to put weak object pronouns between θα and the verb (e.g., θα το κάνω ). I understand that I must drop the past tense augment (like ε- ) when forming the Simple Future. Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Time & Calendar Up Next: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem (Aorist)

genitive-absolute.title: Unit 32: Participles in the Genitive Absolute (Γενική Απόλυτη)
genitive-absolute.content: Participles in the Genitive Absolute (Γενική Απόλυτη) In Level B2, you learned about basic Participles. But there is a "secret" third type of participle structure—a ghost from Ancient Greek that survived in modern, highly formal Katharevousa-style texts. It is called the Genitive Absolute (Η Γενική Απόλυτη Μετοχή) . You will rarely hear this spoken in a coffee shop, but you will absolutely see it in newspapers, official documents, literature, and news broadcasts. 1. What is the Genitive Absolute? Sometimes you want to connect two actions that happen at the same time, but they have two different subjects. In normal everyday Greek, you use the word ενώ (while) or επειδή (because): Επειδή έβρεχε, μείναμε μέσα. (Because it was raining, we stayed inside). To sound incredibly formal and elevated, C2 speakers rip out the connector words, erase the verb, and replace the whole clause with Nouns and Participles in the Genitive Case . 2. The Formula & The Grammar Instead of a normal sentence with a Nominative subject and a normal verb, you create an "Absolute" bubble out of Genitive words. Formula: [Noun in Genitive] + [Participle in Genitive] A. The Ancient Active Participles (-ων, -ουσα, -ον) To understand absolute expressions, you must recognize a class of ancient active participles that survive in formal Modern Greek. The stem for the Genitive forms ends in -οντ- . Genitive: του τρέχ οντ ος (of the running one). Plural Genitive: των τρέχ οντ ων (of the running ones). B. Passive Participles & Reduplication (-μένος) In highly formal C2 Greek, many passive participles feature an ancient prefix (an extra syllable) called reduplication . δίνω (I give) ➔ δεδομένος (given). τελώ (I perform) ➔ τετελεσμένος (accomplished). TIP Example in Context: Normal Greek: Επειδή αυτά τα πράγματα είναι δεδομένα... Genitive Absolute: Δεδομένων αυτών των πραγμάτων... (These things being given...) 3. The Most Common Frozen Expressions In Modern Greek, people almost exclusively rely on a handful of highly recognizable, frozen expressions. The Genitive Absolute Literal Translation True Meaning Θεού θέλοντος God willing Hopefully / God willing Καλώς εχόντων των πραγμάτων Things having well All being well / Hopefully Απουσιάζοντος του καθηγητού The teacher being absent With the teacher absent Τρέχοντος του έτους The year running During the current year Μη γένοιτο! Let it not happen God forbid! Τουναντίον... Of the opposite On the contrary... Καλώς εχόντων των πραγμάτων, θα τελειώσουμε το έργο αύριο. (All going well, we will finish the project tomorrow). 🎯 Unit 32 Mastery Checklist I know that the Genitive Absolute is an ancient, highly formal way of connecting two clauses. I understand it is built using a Genitive Subject + a Genitive Participle. I know that active participles use the -οντ- stem in the genitive (e.g., θέλ οντ ος). I recognize reduplication in formal passive participles (like δε δομένος). I have memorized the most common C2 expressions: Θεού θέλοντος and Καλώς εχόντων των πραγμάτων . Practice Vocabulary: C2 Vocabulary: Advanced Particles & Absolute Expressions Up Next: Unit 33: Irregular High-Level Expressions

genitive-case.title: Unit 15: The Genitive Case (Possession & Relationships)
genitive-case.content: Unit 15: The Genitive Case (Possession & Relationships) You’ve mastered Subjects (Nominative) and Objects (Accusative). Now, we enter the world of Ownership. The Genitive case is the Greek equivalent of the English apostrophe "s" ('s) or the word "of". 1. The Genitive Articles When a person or thing becomes an "Owner," the definite article must change. There is a very consistent pattern here: Gender Singular Owner Plural Owners Masculine του των Feminine της των Neuter του των TIP The Universal Plural In the Genitive plural, gender doesn't exist! Every single plural owner uses the article των and the noun always ends in -ων . Των φίλ ων , των δασκάλ ων , των παιδι ών . 2. Noun Endings (Singular) How does the noun itself change to show possession? Masculine (-ος, -ας, -ης): Nouns ending in -ας or -ης simply drop the final -ς ( ο πατέρας ➔ του πατέρα ). Nouns in -ος change to -ου . Feminine (-η, -α): Always add a final -ς ( η Μαρία ➔ της Μαρίας ). Neuter (-ο, -ι, -μα): Nouns ending in -ο change to -ου ( το βιβλίο ➔ του βιβλίου ). Nouns ending in -ι change the final vowel to a diphthong -ιού ( το παιδί ➔ του παιδιού ). Nouns ending in -μα actually add an extra syllable, becoming -ματος ( το πρόβλημα ➔ του προβλήματος ). WARNING The Neuter & Feminine "-ος" Traps Neuters in -ος: Remember words like το λάθος (the mistake)? In the Genitive singular, they change their ending to -ους ( το κράτος ➔ του κράτους ). Feminines in -ος: Words like η οδός (the street) behave like masculine nouns and take an -ου ending, but keep their feminine article ( η μέθοδος ➔ της μεθόδου ). TIP Pronunciation Secret: The Shifting Accent The Genitive case is famous for moving the stress of a word! If a noun (masculine or neuter) is stressed on the third syllable from the end, the accent usually moves one syllable to the right in the Genitive. ο άν θρωπος ➔ του αν θρώ που το θέ ατρο ➔ του θε ά τρου 3. Possession & Relationships: The "Of" Rule In English, we say "The teacher's book." In Greek, you must say "The book of the teacher." The object being owned comes first, and both the item and the owner keep their articles . Ο σκύλος του Γιώργου (George's dog / The dog of George). Το σπίτι της Μαρίας (Maria's house / The house of Maria). Το χρώμα του αυτοκινήτου (The car's color / The color of the car). The Genitive isn't just for physical ownership. It is used to express a variety of relationships: Creators and Creations: η δημιουργία του Παύλου (Paul's creation). Age and Measurements: ένα παιδί τεσσάρων χρονών (a four-year-old child). Contents or Quality: στιγμές ευτυχίας (moments of happiness). 4. Indirect Objects & Prepositions As you learned in Unit 13, the Genitive also marks the Receiver of an action (the Indirect Object). Μου δίνεις... (You give to me...). Του λέω... (I am telling him...). The "Big Three" Genitive Prepositions While you learned in Unit 5 that almost all prepositions in Greek take the Accusative case, there are three specific prepositions that force the noun into the Genitive case : 1. εναντίον (against): εναντίον του πολέμου (against the war). 2. εξαιτίας (because of): εξαιτίας της βροχής (because of the rain). 3. μεταξύ (between/among): μεταξύ μας (between us). 🎯 Unit 15 Mastery Checklist I can use the correct Genitive articles: του, της, του (singular) and των (plural). I know that Greek structures possession as "The Item of the Owner". I remember the traps: neuters in -ος become -ους , and the accent often shifts to the right! I know the three prepositions that require the Genitive: εναντίον, εξαιτίας, μεταξύ . Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Family Tree & Possessions Up Next: Unit 16: Imperative Mood (Giving Orders)

idioms.title: Unit 28: Idioms & Ancient Remnants
idioms.content: Unit 28: Idioms & Ancient Remnants To reach true fluency (B2/C1), you must understand the "ghosts" of the past. Modern Greek is filled with ancient expressions and colorful idioms that don't follow modern rules. 1. Ancient Dative Expressions Ancient Greek had a "Dative" case (for/to) that vanished in modern times, but these frozen phrases survived. You must learn them as whole blocks: Εν τω μεταξύ (In the meantime). Δόξα τω Θεώ (Thank God / Glory to God). Εν πάση περιπτώσει (In any case / Anyway). Τοις μετρητοίς (In cash). 2. Advanced Prepositions (Genitive) While basic prepositions take the Accusative, formal/ancient ones used in news and business take the Genitive . Λόγω + Genitive (Due to): Λόγω βροχής (Due to rain). Μέσω + Genitive (Via): Μέσω Αθηνών (Via Athens). Ενώπιον + Genitive (Before / In front of): Ενώπιον του νόμου (Before the law). 3. Colorful Idioms These are phrases where the literal meaning is completely different from the actual meaning. Έγινε της κακομοίρας! (A disaster happened! / Hell broke loose!) Χάνω τα λόγια μου. (I am lost for words). Πού το πας; (What are you getting at? / Where are you going with this?) Περαστικά! (Get well soon! / May it pass!) 4. Compound "Lego" Words Greek builds new words by snapping smaller ones together with the letter "ο" as glue. βιβλίο (book) + πωλείο (shop) ➔ βιβλιοπωλείο (bookstore). ψάρι (fish) + ταβέρνα ➔ ψαροταβέρνα (fish tavern). 🎯 B2 Advanced Capstone Congratulations! You have navigated the complex waters of intermediate and advanced Greek grammar. You have mastered: Advanced Particles and Spoken Nuance. Relative and Reflexive Pronouns. Comparison of Adjectives. Participles and Impersonal Verbs. Formal Ancient Remnants. Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Beliefs, Relations & Society Up Next: Level C: Proficiency (Dialogue & Literature)

imperative.title: Unit 16: The Imperative Mood (Giving Orders)
imperative.content: Unit 16: The Imperative Mood (Giving Orders) The Imperative Mood is used to give positive commands, orders, or strong advice. In Greek, just like in the Future and Past tenses, giving a command is deeply tied to Aspect (Video vs. Photo). The imperative only has 2nd person forms (singular for "you" and plural for "you all" or formal "you"). 1. Simple Commands (The Photo: Do it once!) This is the most common way to give a command. You use the Simple (Perfective) Stem that you learned in Unit 12. Active Simple Commands: Εσύ (Informal): Add -ε to the simple stem (e.g., γράψ- ➔ Γράψε! ). Εσείς (Formal/Plural): The ending depends on the last letter of the stem! If the stem ends in λ, ρ, σ, ξ, or ψ , add -τε (e.g., γράψ- ➔ Γράψτε! ). If it ends in other consonants, add -ετε (e.g., πλύν- ➔ Πλύνετε! ). Note: Verbs ending in 'ν' often take the shorter '-τε' ending in colloquial speech (e.g., Κάντε! - Do!). TIP Pronunciation Secret (Vowel Dropping) Active singular commands ending in λ, ρ, σ, ξ, or ψ often drop their final -ε when followed by a weak pronoun (like το or τα ). Γράψ' το! (Write it!). Φέρ' τα βιβλία! (Bring the books!). Passive Simple Commands: Yes, passive verbs can be commands too! To form the singular, you add -ου to the active perfective stem. The plural is identical to the passive dependent form. Χάσου! (Get lost! / Lose yourself!). Ετοιμάσου! (Get ready!). Ετοιμαστείτε! (Get ready, you all!). 2. Continuous Commands (The Video: Keep doing it!) Used for habits or ongoing actions. You use the Continuous (Imperfective/Present) Stem . There are no passive forms for continuous commands. First-Conjugation Verbs (Type A): Εσύ: Add -ε (e.g., Τρέχε! ). > [!TIP] > For verbs with more than two syllables, the stress jumps back to the third syllable from the end (e.g., Διάβαζε! ). Εσείς: Add -ετε (e.g., Τρέχετε! ). Note: This form is identical to the normal Present tense! . Second-Conjugation Verbs (The Accented Verbs): Type A (-άω): Sg: -α ( Αγάπα! ), Pl: -άτε ( Αγαπάτε! ). Type B (-ώ): They have no singular form ! The plural is identical to the present tense (e.g., Θεωρείτε! ). 3. The Absolute Irregulars Like most languages, the most common commands in Greek are irregular. You MUST memorize these: Λέω (Say) ➔ Πες! / Πείτε! (or Πέστε!) Βλέπω (Look) ➔ Δες! / Δείτε! (or Δέστε!) Βρίσκω (Find) ➔ Βρες! / Βρείτε! Πίνω (Drink) ➔ Πιες! / Πιείτε! (or Πιέστε!) Έρχομαι (Come) ➔ Έλα! / Ελάτε! Πηγαίνω / Πάω (Go) ➔ Πήγαινε! / Πηγαίνετε! 4. Negative Commands (Μην) & The "Για" Particle To say "Don't," you use the magic particle μην (instead of δεν) followed by the Subjunctive form. The word 'να' can optionally be placed before it. Μην κλείσεις την πόρτα. (Don't close the door—this time). (Να) μην τρέχεις. (Don't run—habitually). Note: If you want to shout a single-word command for "Don't!", you just say Μη! . TIP Native Secret: The "Για" Particle If you want to add extra encouragement to a positive command, put the hortatory particle για in front of it! It translates to "Do..." or "Come on...". Για κάτσε! (Do sit down!). Για φέρε μας λίγα παγάκια. (Do bring us some ice cubes). 🎯 Unit 16 Mastery Checklist I can give a simple command using the Simple Stem ( Γράψε ) and know when to use -τε vs -ετε for the plural. I know how to drop the final vowel in phrases like Γράψ' το . I can form continuous commands and know that Type B verbs have no singular continuous command. I have memorized the irregular commands ( Πες, Δες, Βες, Έλα, Πήγαινε ). I know that negative commands use Μην + the Subjunctive, and I can use 'Για' to encourage someone. Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Directions & Commands Up Next: Unit 17: Adverbs & Expressions of Manner

impersonal-verbs.title: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions
impersonal-verbs.content: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions Sometimes a sentence doesn't have a specific "person" doing the action (e.g., "It is raining" or "It is necessary"). In English, we use a dummy "it" as the subject. In Greek, we use Impersonal Verbs which have no subject and are permanently frozen in the 3rd Person Singular. The most basic examples are weather verbs, like βρέχει (it is raining) and χιονίζει (it is snowing). But at the B2 level, impersonal verbs are used to express complex opinions, possibilities, and rules. 1. The Power Pair: Πρέπει & Μπορεί You’ve used these since A1, but now you must understand their advanced native nuances. Both verbs are almost always followed by the subjunctive particle να . Πρέπει να... (It is necessary that... / Must) Μπορεί να... (It is possible that... / Might / Maybe) WARNING Μπορώ vs. Μπορεί Remember that μπορώ can be conjugated when it means "ability", but stays frozen as μπορεί when it means "possibility". Μπορώ να πάω. (I can go—I have the ability). Μπορεί να πάω. (I might go—it is a possibility). Advanced B2 Nuances: 1. Probability: Just like English, Greeks use πρέπει να (must) to express strong probability or deduction about the past, rather than just obligation! Είδα φώτα. Πρέπει να έχουν γυρίσει. (I saw lights. They must have returned). 2. The Negation Trap: Where you put the negative particle with μπορεί changes the meaning completely! Μπορεί να μην έχουν γυρίσει. (They may not have returned). Δεν μπορεί να έχουν γυρίσει. (They can't have returned - It is impossible!). 2. "It's not going to happen": Πρόκειται Along with πρέπει , one of the most common impersonal verbs in Greek is πρόκειται (to be about to / be in question). In everyday speech, it is heavily used in the negative to firmly state that something is simply not going to happen. Δεν πρόκειται να φύγουμε σήμερα. (We're not going to / not about to leave today). Δεν πρόκειται να τον ξεχάσει. (She is not about to forget him). 3. Essential Intermediate Verbs These verbs are frozen in the -ει or -εται forms and describe allowed actions, appearances, or values. Απαγορεύεται (It is forbidden): Απαγορεύεται το κάπνισμα. Επιτρέπεται (It is allowed): Επιτρέπεται να μπεις. Φαίνεται (It seems): Φαίνεται ότι δεν την πιστεύουν. (It seems that they do not believe her). Πειράζει (It matters / It bothers): Τον πειράζει που του μιλάει απότομα. (It bothers him that she speaks to him sharply). Αξίζει (It is worth): Αξίζει να το δεις. TIP Native Secret: Impersonal "Έχει" To say "There is" or "There are", Greeks colloquially use the frozen 3rd person singular verb έχει (literally "it has") instead of υπάρχει ! Έχει πολλούς ανθρώπους εκεί μέσα. ( There are a lot of people in there). 4. Impersonal "Είναι" Expressions You can turn any adjective or noun into a command or observation by using Είναι (It is) followed by να , ότι , or που . Είναι δύσκολο να... (It is difficult to...) Είναι πιθανόν να... (It is likely that...). Είναι φανερό ότι... (It is obvious that...). Είναι κρίμα που... (It is a pity that...). 🎯 Unit 27 Mastery Checklist I know that impersonal verbs are frozen in the 3rd person singular (-ει or -εται) and have no subject. I can use Πρέπει να to express probability ("they must have..."). I understand the massive difference between Δεν μπορεί να (impossible) and Μπορεί να μην (may not). I can firmly deny something using Δεν πρόκειται να... (There's no way / Not about to...). I know the colloquial trick of using Έχει to mean "There is/are". I can use Είναι + Adjective + να/ότι/που to make impersonal observations. Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Expressions, impersonal states & weather Up Next: Unit 28: Idioms & Ancient Remnants

interrogative-pronouns.title: Unit 7: Asking Questions
interrogative-pronouns.content: Unit 7: Asking Questions (Τι, Πού, Ποιος) You now have a solid vocabulary and know how to conjugate verbs. The final step in your A1 foundation is learning how to ask for things. In Greek, this involves two parts: a special punctuation mark and "Question Words" (interrogative pronouns and adverbs). 1. The Greek Question Mark (;) The first thing you must memorize is that the English semicolon (;) is the Greek question mark. The English question mark (?) does not exist in Greek. Be careful not to confuse the two when reading! Πώς είσαι; (How are you?) Τι κάνεις; (What are you doing?) 2. The Fixed "W" Words (Indeclinable) These words are easy because they are indeclinable—they never change their shape regardless of gender, number, or case. Τι (ti) = What? (e.g., Τι είναι αυτό; ) Πού (poo) = Where? (e.g., Πού είσαι; ) Πότε (pó-te) = When? (e.g., Πότε φεύγουμε; ) Πώς (pos) = How? (e.g., Πώς είσαι; ) Γιατί (ya-tí) = Why? (e.g., Γιατί λες όχι; ) TIP Accent Mystery: You learned in Unit 1 that 1-syllable words generally don't get accents. Πού and Πώς are rare exceptions! They keep the accent only to differentiate them from their identical twins που (the relative pronoun "that/who") and πως (the conjunction "that"). (Notice that 'Τι' does not have a twin, so it never takes an accent). 3. The Chameleon "W" Words (Declinable) Just like adjectives, some question words are "chameleons". They must perfectly match the gender, number, and case of the noun you are asking about. "Who" or "Which" (Ποιος) Pronounced as one syllable: pyos . It declines like an adjective with the endings -ος, -α, -ο. Ποιος είναι ο άντρας; (Who is the man?) Ποια είναι η γυναίκα; (Who is the woman?) Ποιο παιδί; (Which child?) IMPORTANT Accusative Alert! If "Who" is the object receiving the action, it drops its -ς and adds a -ν , becoming Ποιον . Ποιον βλέπεις; (Who(m) do you see?) NOTE How to ask "Whose?" To ask "Whose?", you must use the Genitive (possession) case. You can use the formal word Τίνος (for masculine and neuter), or the very common colloquial forms: ποιανού (whose - masc/neut) and ποιανής (whose - fem). Ποιανού είναι αυτό το βιβλίο; (Whose book is this?) "How Much" or "How Many" (Πόσος) This word declines like an adjective with the endings -ος, -η, -ο. Πόσο κάνει; (How much does it cost? - Neuter ) Πόση ζάχαρη θέλεις; (How much sugar do you want? - Feminine ) Πόσα παιδιά έχεις; (How many children do you have? - Neuter plural ) 4. The "Preposition First" Rule In English, you can end a question with a preposition (e.g., "Where did you buy those shoes from ?" or "Who are you talking to ?"). In Greek, you can NEVER separate the preposition from the question word . The preposition must always come immediately before the "W" word: Από πού τα αγόρασες; ( From where did you buy them?) Με ποιον μιλάς; ( With whom are you speaking?) 5. Word Order & Intonation To make a statement into a Yes/No question, you don't need to invert the subject and the verb like in English (e.g., He is -> Is he? ). You often just change the punctuation to ( ; ) and use a questioning tone of voice. Statement: Ο Γιώργος είναι εδώ. (George is here.) Question: Ο Γιώργος είναι εδώ; (Is George here?) 🎯 A1 Foundations Capstone Congratulations! You have officially completed the A1 Stage . You have mastered: The Writing System (Alphabet, Digraphs, Accents). Noun Basics (Genders, Articles, Cases). Fundamental Verbs (Being, Having, Type A Verbs). Building basic negative sentences and asking questions properly. Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Question Words & Pronouns Up Next: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives

irregular-words.title: Unit 33: Irregular & Defective Words
irregular-words.content: Irregular & Defective Words You have mastered the rules of Greek grammar. You know how to decline nouns, conjugate verbs, and build complex sentences. But every language has its rebels—words that refuse to follow the rules, and words that are "defective" (missing pieces!). 1. Nouns with Multiple Personalities (Double Plurals) Some nouns change gender when they become plural, or offer two different plural forms with distinct meanings. The Gender-Benders (Masculine to Neuter) Several common masculine nouns ending in -ος become Neuter ending in -α in the plural. Often, both plural forms exist but mean different things: Ο χρόνος (year/time) ➔ Τα χρόνια (years). (Use "οι χρόνοι" only for grammar tenses!) . Ο λόγος (reason/word) ➔ Τα λόγια (the words). ("Οι λόγοι" = the reasons/speeches) . Ο αδελφός (brother) ➔ Τα αδέλφια (siblings). ("Οι αδελφοί" = specifically brothers) . Ο δεσμός (bond) ➔ Οι δεσμοί (relationships) vs. Τα δεσμά (literal shackles). The "A-La-Carte" Plurals (-ούδες) Loanwords and words ending in stressed vowels often add an extra syllable in the plural: Η αλεπού (fox) ➔ οι αλεπούδες . Η μαϊμού (monkey) ➔ οι μαϊμούδες . Η γλωσσού (gossip) ➔ οι γλωσσούδες . 2. Nouns that Refuse to Decline (Indeclinables) Foreign loanwords often remain frozen. They rely entirely on the Article to show case and number. Το σπορ (The sport) ➔ Βλέπω τα σπορ (I see the sports). Η τζαζ (jazz), η ελίτ (the elite), το ασανσέρ (the lift). Common Gender: ο/η ρεπόρτερ, ο/η μάνατζερ. NOTE Even the letters of the Greek alphabet are neuter indeclinable nouns, like το άλφα and το βήτα ! 3. Defective Verbs (Missing Tenses) A "defective" verb (ελλειπτικό ρήμα) exists exclusively in the imperfective aspect —it lacks a Simple Past (Aorist) or Simple Future. ανήκω (I belong). οφείλω (I owe / I am obliged). τρέμω (I tremble). ξέρω (I know) - Has the imperfect ήξερα , but no Simple Past! Impersonal Ghosts Πρέπει (must) and Πρόκειται (is about to) are frozen in the 3rd person singular. Επρόκειτο: The formal past (Imperfect) of Πρόκειται . 4. The Verb "Είδα" & Ancient Remnants While είδα (I saw) is a standard irregular verb, its ancient command form Ιδού (Behold) survives as a frozen remnant. TIP Famous Proverb: "Ιδού η Ρόδος, ιδού και το πήδημα!" (Behold Rhodes, behold the jump!) — meaning: "Prove it right here, right now!" 🎯 Unit 33 Mastery Checklist I know that some masculine nouns change to Neuter in the plural, often changing their meaning (e.g., λόγια vs. λόγοι ). I understand that loanwords like το σπορ and alphabet letters are indeclinable. I recognize defective verbs that strictly lack perfective tenses ( ανήκω , ξέρω ). I can identify frozen ancient remnants like the command Ιδού . ← Unit 32: Genitive Absolute MENU

nouns.title: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns & Articles
nouns.content: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns & Articles Now that you can read Greek, it's time to build sentences. In Greek, nouns (people, places, things) are "alive"—they change their shape based on Gender , Case , and Number . The most important rule for any Greek student is this: IMPORTANT The Golden Rule Never learn a Greek noun by itself. Always memorize it with its article ( ο, η, το ). The article tells you the gender and how the rest of the sentence must behave. 1. Grammatical Gender Every noun in Greek is either Masculine, Feminine, or Neuter. Usually, the last letter of the word tells you the gender. However, grammatical gender does not always correspond to biological sex . For example, the words for "girl" ( το κορίτσι ) and "boy" ( το αγόρι ) are actually grammatically neuter! Gender Article ("The") Common Ending Example Translation Masculine ο -ος, -ας, -ης ο φίλος (o fílos) the (male) friend Feminine η -α, -η η αγάπη (ee agápi) the love Neuter το -ο, -ι, -μα το σπίτι (to spíti) the house TIP Heads up! The tricky "-ος" ending While most nouns ending in -ος are masculine, this is not a universal rule. Some very common ones are feminine (like η οδός - the street, or η μέθοδος - the method). Additionally, many neuter nouns also end in -ος (like το λάθος - the mistake, or το κράτος - the state). Always check the article! 2. Using "A" or "An" (Indefinite Articles) When talking about a non-specific thing (a car, a house), use the indefinite article. Notice how the endings often match the noun! Masculine: ένας ( ένας φίλος ) - a friend Feminine: μία / μια ( μια αγάπη ) - a love Neuter: ένα ( ένα σπίτι ) - a house NOTE Pronunciation & Spelling Tip: The feminine μια is pronounced as a single syllable, while μία is pronounced as two syllables and is generally used when you want to emphasize the number "one". For the masculine and feminine object forms, έναν and μιαν may optionally drop their final "-ν" depending on the word that follows them. 3. Intro to Cases: Subject vs. Object In English, "The man" is always "The man," whether he is doing the action or receiving it. In Greek, there are four cases, meaning the word changes shape depending on its role in the sentence. Today we focus on the two most important: The Nominative (The Subject) The form you find in the dictionary. Used for the person or thing performing the action or being described. Ο πατέρας είναι εδώ.
