(The father is here) The Accusative (The Object) Used for the person or thing receiving the action (the Direct Object). Rule for Masculine: Drop the final -ς . The article becomes τον . ( Ο πατέρας -> τον πατέρα ). Rule for Feminine: The noun's ending doesn't change. The article becomes τη(ν) . Rule for Neuter: No changes at all! Neuter nouns and articles ( το ) are exactly the same in both the Subject and Object roles. Case Masculine Feminine Neuter Subject (Nom) ο πατέρας η μητέρα το παιδί Object (Acc) τον πατέρα τη(ν) μητέρα το παιδί TIP The "τη(ν)" Rule: The feminine article 'την' drops its 'ν' before most consonants. You must keep the 'ν' only if the next word starts with a vowel, or with the hard sounds κ, π, τ, γκ, μπ, ντ, ξ, ψ . (e.g., τη Δευτέρα - on Monday, but την άνοιξη - in the spring). 4. When to use the Definite Article ("The") Greek uses the definite article much more often than English! Names take articles: You don't say "Maria is here." You say " Η Μαρία είναι εδώ. " (The Maria is here). Abstract ideas & Classes: Greek uses "the" for abstract concepts and general categories. You don't say "Fear is an instinct", you say " Ο φόβος είναι ένστικτο " (The fear is an instinct). 🎯 Unit 2 Mastery Checklist I can identify the general gender of a noun by its ending, but I know to watch out for "-ος" exceptions. I know the definite articles ( ο, η, το ) and indefinite articles ( ένας, μια, ένα ) by heart. I understand that masculine nouns drop the -ς when they are the object of a sentence. I know the rule for when to keep or drop the final -ν in the feminine article τη(ν) . I remember to put definite articles in front of people's names! Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Family & People Up Next: Unit 3: Being & Having

object-pronouns.title: Unit 13: Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns (Weak Pronouns)
object-pronouns.content: Unit 13: Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns (Weak Pronouns) In Unit 3, you learned how to say "I see him" ( Τον βλέπω ). These are Direct Objects . Now, we learn how to handle the receiver of an action: the Indirect Object (e.g., "Give to me "). These short, unstressed pronouns are formally known as "weak" or "clitic" pronouns. Mastering them is essential for natural, everyday Greek conversation. 1. The Two Types of Objects Direct Object (Accusative Case) The direct target of the action. Με βλέπεις; (Do you see me?). Τον ξέρω. (I know him.) Indirect Object (Genitive Case) The person receiving something, or to whom/for whom an action is done. In English, we usually add "to" or "for", but in Greek, we simply use the Genitive case form of the pronoun! Μου δίνεις... (You give to me...) Σου λέω... (I am telling to you...) English Direct (Accusative) Indirect (Genitive) Me / To me με μου You / To you (sg.) σε σου Him / To him τον του Her / To her τη(ν) της It / To it το του We / To us μας μας You / To you (pl.) σας σας Them (m/f/n) τους / τις / τα τους TIP Notice the Plurals! For "We" ( μας ) and "You plural" ( σας ), the Accusative and Genitive forms are exactly the same! 2. Position in the Sentence These weak pronouns almost always sit immediately in front of the verb . Nothing can separate them from the verb except other weak pronouns. Statement: Σου το δίνω. (I am giving it to you). With Particles: They sit safely between the particles ( δεν, μην, θα, να, ας ) and the verb. Δεν σου το δίνω. (I am not giving it to you). Θα μου το πεις; (Will you tell it to me?). WARNING The Imperative Exception There is a major exception to the "front" rule. When you use an Imperative (a command) or a Gerund (an "-ing" word), the weak pronouns move to the end of the verb! Normal: Μου το λες. (You tell it to me). Command: Πες μου το . (Tell it to me!). Pronunciation Tip (Enclisis): If adding the pronoun to the end of a command pushes the stress more than three syllables back, the verb gets an extra accent mark ! (e.g., Κοίταξέ με! - Look at me!). 3. Stacking Pronouns (The Double Pronoun Rule) What if you want to say "I give it (the book) to her (Maria)"? In Greek, the person always comes before the thing. The rule is strict: The Indirect Object (Genitive) always comes BEFORE the Direct Object (Accusative) . Formula: Indirect (Gen) + Direct (Acc) + Verb Της το δίνω. (I give it to her). Μου το λες; (Are you telling it to me?). Σας τα γράφω. (I am writing them [neut] to you all). Μη μου τα δείξεις. (Don't show them to me). TIP Native Speaker Secrets: The "S' to" blend: In rapid colloquial speech, the combination σου το is often squeezed together into a single sound: σ' το ! (e.g., Σ' το είπα! - I told you it!). Flexible Commands: While the Genitive + Accusative order is perfectly strict before a verb, when they attach to the end of a command, Greeks are flexible! You can say either Πες του το OR Πες το του (Tell him it / Tell it him). 🎯 Unit 13 Mastery Checklist I know the difference between με (Accusative/Direct) and μου (Genitive/Indirect). I have learned the plural forms ( μας, σας, τους ). I remember that the person (Indirect) comes before the thing (Direct): Της το δίνω. I know that pronouns go after the verb for commands ( Πες μου ). I can negate a double-pronoun sentence: Δεν σου το λέω. Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Shopping & Clothes Up Next: Unit 14: The Past Tenses (Imperfect vs. Aorist)

participles.title: Unit 26: Active & Passive Participles (Οι Μετοχές)
participles.content: Unit 26: Active & Passive Participles (Οι Μετοχές) A Participle is a verb dressed up to act like an adjective or an adverb. In English, we use -ing (Running) or -ed (Locked). Greek has two powerful forms you must master at the B2 level: the Active Participle (Gerund) and the Passive Perfect Participle. 1. The Active Participle / Gerund (-οντας / -ώντας) This is an uninflected (frozen) verb form used as an adverb. It translates to the English "-ing" form. It is used to show manner, simultaneous time (two actions happening at the same time), and occasionally cause. Formula: It is formed by adding a suffix to the continuous (imperfective) stem. τρέχω ➔ τρέχοντας (while running / by running). παίζω ➔ παίζοντας (while playing). γελώ ➔ γελώντας (while laughing). WARNING Spelling & Stress Rule (-ο vs -ω): How do you know whether to write -οντας (with omicron) or -ώντας (with omega)? It all depends on the stress! If the stress falls on the stem , use -ο- (e.g., τρέχ ο ντας, παίζ ο ντας). If the stress falls on the ending (like Type B verbs), use -ώ- (e.g., αγαπ ώ ντας, γελ ώ ντας). TIP The Frozen Ending The active participle is indeclinable. It never changes to match gender or number! Ο αδελφός της ήρθε τρέχοντας. (Her brother came running). 2. The Passive Perfect Participle (-μένος) Unlike the gerund, the passive perfect participle acts exactly like a normal adjective. It describes a completed state resulting from an action (e.g., Tired, Broken, Hidden). Because it is an adjective, it must perfectly agree with the noun it modifies in gender, number, and case. κουράζω (tire) ➔ κουρασμένος (tired). χάνω (lose) ➔ χαμένος (lost). Η πόρτα είναι κλεισμένη. (The door is closed). Τα χαμένα λόγια. (The wasted/lost words). TIP Advanced Tip: The Consonant "Cheat Codes" The passive participle is formed from the passive perfective stem . If you remember the consonant groups from Unit 12 and 18, you can predict the exact spelling! The 'S' Group (ζω, νω, θω): Uses -σμένος (e.g., ξεχνώ ➔ ξεχα σμ ένος). The 'PS' (Lip) Group (πω, βω, φω): The lips close completely, becoming -μμένος (e.g., γράφω ➔ γρα μμ ένος, κρύβω ➔ κρυ μμ ένος). The 'X' (Throat) Group (κω, γω, χω): The throat sound softens to -γμένος (e.g., ανοίγω ➔ ανοι γμ ένος, τραβώ ➔ τραβη γμ ένος). IMPORTANT Native Secret: The "Emotions" Exception Intransitive verbs (verbs without a direct object) normally do not have passive forms. However, some common verbs related to physical or emotional states do have passive perfect participles! πεινώ (I am hungry) ➔ πεινασμένος (hungry). διψώ (I am thirsty) ➔ διψασμένος (thirsty). θυμώνω (I get angry) ➔ θυμωμένος (angry). αρρωσταίνω (I get sick) ➔ αρρωστημένος (ill). 3. Formal vs. Informal (-ευμένος vs -εμένος) Verbs ending in -εύω have two participle options. The formal version ends in -ευμένος and the informal, everyday version ends in -εμένος . παντρεύω (marry) ➔ παντρεμένος (informal/common) / παντρευμένος (formal). μπερδεύω (confuse) ➔ μπερδεμένος (confused). 🎯 Unit 26 Mastery Checklist I can use the active participle (gerund) to show simultaneous actions or manner. I know the spelling rule: -οντας when the stress is on the stem, -ώντας when the stress is on the ending. I know that the active participle is frozen and never changes its ending. I can use -μένος as an adjective to describe a completed state. I know that -μένος must match the noun perfectly in gender, number, and case. I can predict spellings like -μμένος and -γμένος based on the verb's consonant group. Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Action & States Up Next: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions

particles.title: Unit 22: Advanced Particles & Spoken Expressions
particles.content: Unit 22: Advanced Particles & Spoken Expressions To sound like a native, you need to master the tiny, untranslatable words that Greeks sprinkle into their speech. Greek has a variety of very important little words called particles, which are highly versatile and often do not correspond precisely to any words in English. These "conversational particles" add flavor, doubt, sarcasm, or intense emotion to any sentence. 1. Curiosity & Doubt Άραγε (I wonder...) Placed at the beginning or end of a question. When used in yes/no questions, άραγε reduces the directness of the question and makes it sound less abrupt and perhaps more polite. Άραγε θα έρθει η Μαρία; (I wonder if Maria will come?) Πού να πήγε, άραγε ; (Where can he have gone, I wonder?). TIP Bonus: Μήπως (By any chance...) Similar to άραγε , you can use μήπως (could it be the case that / by any chance) to soften a question and reduce its directness. Μήπως σε ενόχλησε; (Did he bother you, by any chance?). Τάχα & Δήθεν (Supposedly / "Yeah, right") Used to show extreme sarcasm, pretense, or disbelief. Κλαίει δήθεν επειδή χτύπησε. (He is supposedly crying [but I know he's faking]). Ναι, τάχα μου! (Yeah, right! / Allegedly!) 2. Wishes & Hopes Μακάρι (If only / Let's hope) Triggers the Subjunctive ( να ) or stands alone as a hopeful response. Μακάρι να περάσω τις εξετάσεις! (I hope I pass!) Person A: Θα βρούμε τραπέζι; ➔ Person B: Μακάρι! (Let's hope!) Αχ (Oh! / If only!) This exclamation of emotion is often combined with να and the imperfect tense to express an unfulfilled wish. Αχ, να σ' έβλεπα! (Oh, if only I could see you!). 3. Direction & Commands Ας (Let's / Let it be) Like να , this particle triggers the Subjunctive, but unlike να , it is strictly used only in main clauses. In comparison with να , expressions with ας tend to be much less pressing, because ας combines the sense of a command with permission, or even indifference. Ας πάμε! (Let's go!) Ας έρθουν αν θέλουν. (Let them come if they want [permission/indifference]). TIP The Past Regret You can also use ας with the imperfect tense to express a wish that something had happened (or not happened) in the past. Ας μην τον άκουγες. (If only you hadn't listened to him / You shouldn't have listened to him). Για (The Hortatory Particle) This is a native secret! The hortatory particle για is used colloquially to express extra encouragement right before an imperative (command) form of the verb. It translates roughly to "Come on" or "Do...". Για κάτσε! (Do sit down!). Για ρώτα κανένα δάσκαλο. (Do ask a teacher). Άντε / Άιντε (Come on!) The most versatile word in Greek. It can mean "Let's go!", "Hurry up!", or "Get out of here!" Άντε , σήκω! (Come on, get up!) Άντε πάλι... (Oh, here we go again...) 4. Emotional Interjections Μπράβο! (Well done! / Good!). Α μπα; (Really? / No way!) Αλίμονο! (Alas! / Of course! - depending on context). 🎯 Unit 22 Mastery Checklist I know that particles are versatile words that don't always translate directly into English. I can use άραγε and μήπως to soften a question and express polite curiosity. I know that μακάρι expresses a strong wish. I understand the sarcastic tone of δήθεν and τάχα . I understand that ας is less pressing than να and can express indifference. I know how to use για before a command to offer extra encouragement! Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Conversational Fillers & Particles Up Next: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns (που, ο οποίος)

perfect-tenses.title: Unit 20: The Perfect Tenses (I have done)
perfect-tenses.content: Unit 20: The Perfect Tenses (I have done) In Greek, the Perfect tenses (Present Perfect, Past Perfect/Pluperfect, and Future Perfect) are built almost exactly like in English. You use an auxiliary (helper) verb "to have" combined with a special, unchanging action word. 1. The Helper Verb: Έχω You already know how to conjugate έχω . In these tenses, it acts as the "motor" that drives the action. Depending on the timeline, you will use its Present or Past (Imperfect) forms. Pronoun Present (I have) Past (I had) Εγώ έχω είχα Εσύ έχεις είχες Αυτός/ή/ό έχει είχε Εμείς έχουμε είχαμε Εσείς έχετε είχατε Αυτοί/ές/ά έχουν(ε) είχαν(ε) 2. The Frozen Word (The Non-finite / Απαρέμφατο) To create the action word (e.g., "played", "read", "written"), you take the Simple Stem (from Unit 12) and add a specific ending. This special verb form is called the non-finite (απαρέμφατο). It is completely "frozen"—it never changes regardless of the person. Active Voice (-ει): For active verbs, add the ending -ει to the active simple stem. The stress always falls on the penultimate (last but one) syllable. παίζω ➔ παίξ- ➔ παίξει γράφω ➔ γράψ- ➔ γράψει διαβάζω ➔ διαβάσ- ➔ διαβάσει Passive & Deponent Voice (-εί): The perfect tenses exist in both active and passive voices. To form the passive non-finite, add -εί to the passive perfective stem (the one that usually has a θ, στ, φτ, or χτ ). Notice that the stress always falls on the final syllable! . Deponent verbs (like κοιμάμαι ) only have this passive form, even though their meaning is active. χάνομαι (be lost) ➔ χαθ- ➔ χαθεί . κοιμάμαι (sleep) ➔ κοιμηθ- ➔ κοιμηθεί . WARNING The 1-Syllable Irregulars The irregular verbs that have a 1-syllable dependent form (like δω, πω, βρω ) create a 1-syllable non-finite word. βρίσκω ➔ βρει βλέπω ➔ δει λέω ➔ πει 3. Assembling the Present Perfect Simply put the helper verb and the frozen word together. It is used to express an action completed in the past that has relevance to the present. Έχω παίξει. (I have played). Έχουν διαβάσει. (They have read). Έχεις κοιμηθεί; (Have you slept?). IMPORTANT The Pronoun Rule If you want to use weak object pronouns ("it", "him", "to me"), they must be placed immediately before the appropriate form of έχω . If the sentence is negative, they go right after δεν . Το έχω διαβάσει. (I have read it ). Δεν μας έχει μιλήσει. ((S)he hasn't spoken to us ). 4. Past & Future Perfect To change the timeline, you only change the helper verb έχω . The frozen word stays exactly the same! Past Perfect / Pluperfect (I had done) Use the past tense helper είχα to express an action completed before a specific time in the past. Είχα παίξει. (I had played). Είχα χαθεί. (I had been lost / I had lost myself). Future Perfect (I will have done) Use the future particle θα + the present helper έχω . It expresses an action that will be completed by a particular point in the future. Θα έχω παίξει μέχρι αύριο. (I will have played by tomorrow). Pronoun reminder: Θα σου το έχουν στείλει. (They will have sent it to you ). 🎯 Unit 20 Mastery Checklist I can form the active frozen "-ει" word using the simple stem, keeping the stress on the penultimate syllable. I can form the passive/deponent frozen "-εί" word, keeping the stress on the final syllable. I know that the helper verb έχω / είχα is the only part that conjugates. I can translate "I have", "I had", and "I will have" using this formula. I remember to put weak object pronouns before the helper verb έχω (e.g., Το έχω κάνει ). Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Travel & Life Experiences Up Next: Unit 21: Conditionals (The 'If' Clauses)

plurals.title: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives
plurals.content: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives Welcome to the A2 Expansion Stage ! You’ve mastered the singular. Now, it’s time to multiply everything. In Greek, when a noun becomes plural, its Article , its Ending , and its Adjective must all change to match. 1. Masculine Plurals (Οι / Τους) Masculine nouns are the most "active" during pluralization. The ending depends on the singular form. Singular Plural Subject (Nom) Plural Object (Acc) Pattern ο φίλος οι φίλ οι τους φίλ ους -ος ➔ -οι / -ους ο πατέρας οι πατέρ ες τους πατέρ ες -ας ➔ -ες ο μαθητής οι μαθητ ές τους μαθητ ές -ης ➔ -ες WARNING The -οι vs -ους Trap For nouns ending in -ος , the plural "The" is οι (Subject) but becomes τους (Object). The noun ending follows exactly: -οι matches οι , and -ους matches τους . Οι καλ οί φίλ οι είναι εδώ. (The good friends are here - Subject). Βλέπω τους καλ ούς φίλ ους . (I see the good friends - Object). TIP Pro-Tip: The Shifting Accent If a masculine noun in -ος has its accent on the third syllable from the end (like ο άν θρωπος - the person), the accent moves one syllable to the right in the Accusative plural ( τους ανθρώπους ). The "Extra Syllable" Masculines (Imparisyllabic Nouns) Many everyday masculine nouns ending in -άς , -ής , -ές , or -ούς add an extra syllable ( -δες ) in the plural. ο παπάς (the priest) ➔ οι παπάδες . ο μανάβης (the greengrocer) ➔ οι μανάβηδες . ο καφές (the coffee) ➔ οι καφέδες . 2. Feminine Plurals (Οι / Τις) Feminine nouns are much easier. The noun ending never changes between subject and object—only the article flips! Singular Plural Subject (Nom) Plural Object (Acc) η μητέρα / η αγάπη οι μητέρ ες / οι αγάπ ες τις μητέρ ες / τις αγάπ ες CAUTION The Academic Exception (-εις) While everyday words use -ες , many formal or academic nouns ending in -ση , -ξη , or -ψη form their plural with -εις . η λύση (solution) ➔ οι λύσεις . η σκέψη (thought) ➔ οι σκέψεις . (Note: If the word has more than two syllables, the accent moves one syllable forward in the plural: η κυ βέρ νηση ➔ οι κυβερνήσεις ) . The "Extra Syllable" Feminines Just like some masculine nouns, feminine nouns ending in a stressed -ά or -ού add -δες in the plural. η γιαγιά (grandmother) ➔ οι γιαγιάδες . η αλεπού (fox) ➔ οι αλεπούδες . WARNING The Masked Feminines (-ος) Remember η οδός (the street) from Unit 2? Feminine nouns ending in -ος take masculine endings in the plural ( -οι, -ους ), but keep their feminine articles! Subject: Οι οδ οί . Object: Τις οδ ούς . 3. Neuter Plurals (Τα) Neuter nouns are the most stable. Both the article τα and the noun endings remain the exact same whether they are the subject or the object. Singular Plural (Nom & Acc) Pattern το βιβλίο τα βιβλί α -ο ➔ -α το παιδί τα παιδι ά -ι ➔ -ια το μάθημα τα μαθήμα τα -μα ➔ -ματα IMPORTANT The Ultimate Trap: Neuters in -ος There is a huge group of neuter nouns ending in -ος (like το λάθος - the mistake, το κράτος - the state). In the plural, they drop the -ος and add -η . Do not confuse these with masculine nouns! το λάθος ➔ τα λάθ η . το δάσος (the forest) ➔ τα δάσ η . 4. Adjective & Verb Agreement When the subject is plural, the adjective and the verb must also be plural. Adjectives perfectly match the gender and case of the noun. Singular: Ο καλ ός φίλ ος διαβάζ ει . (The good friend reads). Plural: Οι καλ οί φίλ οι διαβάζ ουν . (The good friends read). Neuter Plural: Τα μικρ ά παιδι ά παίζ ουν . (The small children play). 🎯 Unit 8 Mastery Checklist I can pluralize the articles depending on their case: οι/τους , οι/τις , and τα . I know that masculine -ος nouns change to -οι (subject) and -ους (object). I know about the extra syllable ( -δες ) for nouns like παπάς and γιαγιά . I can correctly pluralize neuter nouns ending in -μα ( μαθήματα ) and -ος ( λάθη ). Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Shopping & Clothes Up Next: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2

reflexive-pronouns.title: Unit 24: Reflexive Pronouns (Myself)
reflexive-pronouns.content: Unit 24: Reflexive Pronouns (Myself) Reflexive expressions in Greek are used to express a situation in which the subject of the sentence is both the actor of the verb and the patient (the receiver). There are three ways of achieving this in Greek: by the reflexive expression ο εαυτός μου (myself), by a passive verb form, or by the verbal prefix αυτο- attached to a passive verb. 1. The Formula (Ο εαυτός μου) The word εαυτός (self) is a masculine noun. To say "myself," you simply add the weak possessive pronoun after it. Person Greek English 1st (Singular) τον εαυτό μου myself 2nd (Singular) τον εαυτό σου yourself 3rd (Singular) τον εαυτό του / της himself / herself 1st (Plural) τους εαυτούς μας ourselves 2nd (Plural) τους εαυτούς σας yourselves 3rd (Plural) τους εαυτούς τους themselves TIP Native Secret: The Singular Plural You might logically think that "ourselves" or "themselves" should always use the plural form ( τους εαυτούς μας/τους ). However, the definite article and the noun εαυτός are normally in the singular even when the possessive pronoun is in the plural! Μόνο τον εαυτό τους κοιτάζουν. (They only look after themselves). 2. How to Use them The definite article ο and the noun εαυτός appear most frequently in the Accusative case when the reflexive expression is the direct object of the verb. Αγαπώ τον εαυτό μου. (I love myself). Προσέχεις τον εαυτό σου; (Do you take care of yourself?). The reflexive expression may also occur in a prepositional phrase. Πρέπει να έχεις εμπιστοσύνη μόνο στον εαυτό σου. (You should only trust yourself). Η Μαρία έχει μεγάλη ιδέα για τον εαυτό της. (Mary has a very high opinion of herself). 3. Reflexive Verbs vs. Pronouns In Unit 18, you learned that Passive Verbs (-ομαι) are often used reflexively. The passive verb form expresses reflexivity for some verbs of grooming or bodily care, especially when the subject is human. Πρέπει να ντύνεσαι ζεστά τον χειμώνα. (You must dress [yourself] warm in the winter). Πάλι χτενίζεσαι; (Are you combing your hair again?). So when do you use the pronoun? You use the pronoun phrase ( τον εαυτό μου ) for special emphasis, or with verbs that do not inherently imply bodily care or do not have a reflexive passive form: Βλάπτω τον εαυτό μου. (I am hurting myself). 4. The Advanced "Αυτο-" Prefix A few reflexives can be formed with the prefix αυτο- attached to the passive verb. This is a true mark of B2/C1 fluency and is often used in news and literature! Ο άνθρωπος αυτός αυτοκαταστράφηκε. (This man destroyed himself). 🎯 Unit 24 Mastery Checklist I can form the reflexive phrase for any person (e.g., τον εαυτό της). I know that εαυτός is a masculine noun and is normally kept in the singular even for plural subjects ( τον εαυτό μας ). I know how to use the reflexive phrase inside a prepositional phrase ( στον εαυτό σου ). I understand when to use the pronoun instead of a passive verb ending (grooming vs. emphasis). I recognize the prefix αυτο- as a reflexive marker. Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Beliefs, Relations & Society Up Next: Unit 25: Adjective Comparison (Better, Faster)

relative-pronouns.title: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns (Who, Which, That)
relative-pronouns.content: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns (Who, Which, That) Relative pronouns link two sentences together. Instead of saying "I saw a man. The man was tall," you say "I saw a man who was tall." In Greek, we have two ways to do this: the casual "easy" way and the formal way. 1. The Easy Way: Που The word που is the ultimate chameleon. It is an indeclinable relative particle, meaning it never changes shape regardless of the noun's gender, number, or whether it refers to a human or an object. It can mean who, which, that, or where. Ο άντρας που είδα... (The man [whom] I saw...) Το σπίτι που ζήσαμε... (The house [that/where] we lived...) Το δέντρο που είδες... (The tree [that] you saw...) WARNING Spelling Alert: που vs. πού; Don't confuse που (that/which) with πού; (Where?). The question word always has an accent; the relative pronoun never does. TIP Native Secret: The "Resumptive" Pronoun When που refers to an object (especially an indirect object), Greeks frequently insert an optional weak pronoun inside the relative clause to clarify the meaning. Η γιατρός που μου τη σύστησες. (The (female) doctor who you recommended her to me / whom you recommended). Ο νεαρός που του δώσανε την υποτροφία. (The young man who they gave him the scholarship / to whom they gave the scholarship). 2. The Formal Way: Ο οποίος If you are writing a formal letter or reading the news, you will see the relative pronoun phrase ο οποίος . Unlike που , this phrase consists of the definite article followed by the word οποίος . It acts like an adjective and must perfectly match the noun in Gender and Number, but its Case depends on its syntactic role in its own relative clause. Ο άντρας ο οποίος... (Masc. Nom) Η γυναίκα η οποία... (Fem. Nom) Το παιδί το οποίο... (Neut. Nom) TIP Advanced Tip: How to say "Whose" To say "whose", you simply use the Genitive case form of this pronoun ( του οποίου, της οποίας, των οποίων ). It can be placed either before or after the noun it possesses. Ο φίλος μου, του οποίου η μητέρα είναι δασκάλα... (My friend, whose mother is a teacher...). Ο φίλος μου, η μητέρα του οποίου είναι δασκάλα... (My friend, the mother of whom is a teacher...). 3. Using Prepositions When you need to use prepositions like "with," "from," or "in" before the relative pronoun, the formal ο οποίος is strongly preferred. The relative pronoun must immediately follow the preposition. Αυτοί είναι οι άνθρωποι από τους οποίους πήραμε πληροφορίες. (These are the people from whom we got information). Το κτήριο δίπλα στο οποίο είναι η εκκλησία. (The building next to which is the church). In casual speech, Greeks bypass this completely by using που plus a prepositional phrase with a weak pronoun later in the sentence! Η κοπέλα που κάθεται κοντά της ο Νίκος... (The girl who Nick is sitting near her ...). 4. Free Relatives ("Whoever" & "Whatever") When the relative clause doesn't modify a specific noun, Greek uses "free" correlative pronouns. Όποιος, όποια, όποιο: Whoever. (e.g., Όποιος θέλει... - Whoever wants...). Ό,τι: Whatever or "that which". (e.g., Κάνε ό,τι θέλεις. - Do whatever you want). WARNING Spelling Alert: ότι vs. ό,τι Notice the comma in ό,τι (whatever). It is mandatory in writing to distinguish it from the complementizer ότι (that)! If you want to sound even more emphatic, add the suffix -δήποτε . Οποιοσδήποτε: Anyone / Whoever. Οτιδήποτε: Anything / Whatever. 5. "Of the kind that" (Που να) If the main clause is negative, interrogative, or expresses a wish, the relative clause is often introduced by που να (or ο οποίος να ) followed by the subjunctive mood. It means "of the kind that". Δεν ξέρω κανέναν που να μην αγαπάει τα λεφτά. (I don't know anyone who doesn't like money). Θέλω ένα φίλο στον οποίο / που να μπορώ να έχω εμπιστοσύνη. (I want a friend whom I can trust). 🎯 Unit 23 Mastery Checklist I can use που to link two basic sentences and I know how to add a "resumptive" weak pronoun. I know the difference between που (that), πού; (where?), and που να (of the kind that). I recognize ο οποίος as the formal relative pronoun and can use its Genitive form ( του οποίου ) for "whose." I can use όποιος (whoever) and ό,τι (whatever), and remember to write ό,τι with a comma. Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Beliefs, Relations & Society Up Next: Unit 24: Reflexive Pronouns (Myself)

simple-stem.title: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem (Σ, Ξ, Ψ)
simple-stem.content: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem (Σ, Ξ, Ψ) To speak in the Future or the Subjunctive, you need the Simple (Perfective) Stem . This is the "Photo" stem, used for single, completed actions. Finding this stem is like a puzzle: you look at the ending of the verb's Continuous (Present) stem and swap it for a new one. There are three main "Cheat Codes" to master for Type A (First-Conjugation) verbs. 1. The "S" Group (Σ) ➔ Most common If the present stem ends in a dental consonant (like -θω ), a nasal consonant (like -νω ), the letter -ζω , or simply a vowel (like -ούω or -ύω), the stem usually changes to a -σ- . διαβάζω (read) ➔ διαβάσ- κλείνω (close) ➔ κλείσ- νιώθω (feel) ➔ νιώσ- ακούω (hear) ➔ ακούσ- 2. The "X" Group (Ξ) ➔ Throat sounds If the verb ends in a velar "throat" sound like -κω, -γω, -χω, or -σκω , it explodes into a -ξ- (Xi). ανοίγω (open) ➔ ανοίξ- τρέχω (run) ➔ τρέξ- διδάσκω (teach) ➔ διδάξ- WARNING The -ζω Exceptions! While most verbs ending in -ζω go to the "S" group, some very common ones actually belong to this "X" group. You must memorize these: παίζω (play) ➔ παίξ- αλλάζω (change) ➔ αλλάξ- φωνάζω (shout/call) ➔ φωνάξ- κοιτάζω (look at) ➔ κοιτάξ- 3. The "PS" Group (Ψ) ➔ Lip sounds If the verb ends in a labial "lip" sound like -πω, -βω, -φω , or -πτω , it explodes into a -ψ- (Psi). γράφω (write) ➔ γράψ- λείπω (be absent) ➔ λείψ- κρύβω (hide) ➔ κρύψ- TIP The -εύω / -αύω Rule When a verb ends in -εύω or -αύω , the 'υ' transforms into a lip sound! These verbs also take the -ψ- ending. δουλεύω (work) ➔ δουλέψ- παύω (stop) ➔ πάψ- 4. Why does this matter? Now that you have the perfective stem (e.g., γράψ- ), you simply add the standard present tense endings (-ω, -εις, -ει, etc.). You can use it for everything you learned in Units 10 and 11: Future (Θα): Θα γράψω. (I will write—once). Subjunctive (Να): Θέλω να γράψω. (I want to write—once). IMPORTANT Stress Rule For regular Type A verbs, the stress falls on the last syllable of the perfective stem (θα γρ ά ψω, να δι α βάσω). 5. The "1-Syllable Rebels" (Irregulars) Some verbs are complete rebels. Their perfective stems shrink down to just a single consonant or syllable. Because the stem is so short, the stress moves to the ending (e.g., θα δ ώ , να π ει ς). βλέπω (see) ➔ δ- (θα δώ) λέω (say) ➔ π- (θα πώ) πίνω (drink) ➔ πι- (θα πιώ) βρίσκω (find) ➔ βρ- (θα βρώ) βγαίνω (go out) ➔ βγ- (θα βγώ) μπαίνω (go in) ➔ μπ- (θα μπώ) πάω (go) ➔ πα- (θα πάω) έρχομαι (come) ➔ ερθ- (θα έρθω) 🎯 Unit 12 Mastery Checklist I can turn διαβάζω into διαβάσ- and ακούω into ακούσ- (The S Group). I can turn ανοίγω into ανοίξ- , and I know the exception παίζω ➔ παίξ- (The X Group). I can turn γράφω into γράψ- and δουλεύω into δουλέψ- (The PS Group). I have memorized the "1-Syllable Rebels" ( δώ, πώ, βρώ, πιώ, κτλ. ) and know their stress falls on the ending. Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Food & the Restaurant Up Next: Unit 13: Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns

subjunctive-1.title: Unit 10: Verbal Aspect & The Subjunctive (Να)
subjunctive-1.content: Unit 10: Verbal Aspect & The Subjunctive (Να) Welcome to the great divide in the Greek language. To move from A1 to A2, you must understand Aspect . Greek doesn’t just care about when something happens (Tense); it deeply cares about how it happens (Aspect). 1. The "Video vs. Photo" Concept (Aspect) Greek forces you to choose between two ways of looking at an action: Continuous (Imperfective) Aspect — The Video: Used for habits, routines, or ongoing background actions that are in progress or repeated. (e.g., "I am playing", "I play every day"). Simple (Perfective) Aspect — The Photo: Used for a single, specific, or completed event viewed in its totality. (e.g., "I want to play one game"). Because of this, every active Greek verb essentially has two stems: Continuous Stem: The one you already know from the Present tense (e.g., παίζ- ). Simple Stem: The "Perfective" stem (e.g., παίξ- ). We will learn exactly how to build this in the next units, but you will start seeing it everywhere now! 2. The Subjunctive Mood (Magic words: Να & Ας) Greek has no "infinitive" form of verbs (like the English "to go" or "to play"). Instead, it uses the subjunctive mood, formed by the particle να followed by a conjugated verb. English Greek (Literal translation) Greek (Actual) I want to go . I want that I go . Θέλω να πάω . You want to play . You want that you play . Θέλεις να παίξεις ; When to use Να: After Verbs of Wishing, Ability, & Obligation: This is the most common use. You use it after verbs like θέλω (I want), μπορώ (I can), and the impersonal πρέπει (I must). Deliberative Questions: To ask for instructions or offer something. Να σε ρωτήσω κάτι; (May I ask you something? / Should I ask you something?). Commands & Wishes: Να προσέχεις! (Take care / You should be careful!). TIP Bonus Magic Word: Ας (Let's / Let) While να is the standard subjunctive particle, Greek also uses ας to express suggestions, permission, or indifference. Ας φύγει. (Let him go.) Ας αφήσουμε αυτό το θέμα. (Let's leave this subject.) WARNING The Subjunctive Negation Rule (Μη vs. Δεν) This is a huge rule! Indicative verbs (normal statements) are negated with δεν (I do not want = δεν θέλω). However, any verb introduced by να or ας MUST be negated by μη(ν) . Θέλω να μην έρθει. (I want him not to come.) Να μην το πείτε σε κανέναν. (You shouldn't tell anybody.) (Note: μη(ν) follows the exact same "dropping 'N'" pronunciation rules you learned in Unit 5!) 3. Aspect in the Subjunctive Since να is followed by a verb, you must choose which "Aspect" (Video or Photo) to use for that verb: Θέλω να παίζω (Continuous/Video) : I want to be playing (regularly, as a hobby, or continuously). Θέλω να παίξω (Simple/Photo) : I want to play (this specific game, right now, just once). IMPORTANT The Secret of "Simple" Most of the time, when you ask for something, want to do something, or say you must do something, you are talking about a specific, single event. Therefore, the Simple (Perfective) Stem is by far the most common one used with να ! 🎯 Unit 10 Mastery Checklist I understand the difference between a "Video" (Continuous/Imperfective) and a "Photo" (Simple/Perfective) action. I know that Greek uses να + a conjugated verb instead of an infinitive (e.g., Θέλω να πάω vs Θέλεις να πας). I know how to use ας for "Let / Let's". I remember the golden rule: Subjunctive verbs (after να/ας ) are ALWAYS negated with μη(ν) instead of δεν . Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Routines & B-Group Verbs Up Next: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα)

verb-groups-b.title: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2 (The Accented Verbs)
verb-groups-b.content: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2 (The Accented Verbs) In Unit 4, you learned Type A (First-Conjugation) verbs (like κάνω ), where the accent is fixed on the stem. Now we meet Type B (Second-Conjugation) —verbs where the accent is on the very last vowel of the dictionary form ( -ώ ). These verbs are the "singers" of Greek; they have a distinct rhythm because the stress falls on the endings rather than the stem. They are split into two "vowel teams". 1. Team B1: The "Alpha" Lovers (-άω) These are the most common Type B verbs (known in grammar as Type A of the Second Conjugation). They are characterized by the α vowel in their endings. Examples: αγαπώ (αγαπάω), μιλάω, ρωτάω, ξυπνάω, γελάω . Pronoun Ending Example: μιλάω (speak) (Εγώ) -άω / -ώ μιλάω / μιλώ (Εσύ) -άς μιλάς (Αυτός/ή/ό) -άει / -ά μιλάει / μιλά (Εμείς) -άμε / -ούμε μιλάμε / μιλούμε (Εσείς) -άτε μιλάτε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -άνε / -ούν(ε) μιλάνε / μιλούν(ε) TIP Spoken vs. Written (Formal vs. Informal) You will often see two sets of endings for these verbs. The endings -άω, -άει, -άμε , and -άνε are regarded as less formal and are extremely common in everyday spoken Greek. The alternative forms ( -ώ, -ά, -ούμε, -ούν ) are slightly more formal. For a natural, conversational feel, we recommend sticking to the -άω pattern as your default! 2. Team B2: The "Oo" Lovers (-ώ) These verbs are less common but very important (known in grammar as Type B of the Second Conjugation). They lack the "Alpha" sound and instead use -ει and -ου sounds in their endings. Examples: μπορώ, ζω, τηλεφωνώ, οδηγώ, παρακαλώ . Pronoun Ending Example: μπορώ (can) (Εγώ) -ώ μπορώ (Εσύ) -είς μπορείς (Αυτός/ή/ό) -εί μπορεί (Εμείς) -ούμε μπορούμε (Εσείς) -είτε μπορείτε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ούν(ε) μπορούν(ε) NOTE Notice the Pattern! Except for the first person "I" ( -ώ ) and the "We" and "They" forms, the B2 endings look exactly like the Type A (First-Conjugation) endings you learned in Unit 4 ( -εις, -ει, -ετε, -ουν ), but with an extra vowel "kick" and the accent firmly placed on the ending . 3. How to tell them apart? If a verb has an -άω version in the dictionary or in speech (like μιλάω / μιλώ ), it's B1 . If it ONLY ends in -ώ (like μπορώ ), it's B2 (you can never say "μποράω"). WARNING The "Crossover" Verbs Greek is flexible! Some verbs can actually follow either Team B1 or Team B2. Verbs like τηλεφωνώ (telephone), ακολουθώ (follow), προχωρώ (proceed), and συζητώ (discuss) can be conjugated with B1 endings in casual speech ( τηλεφωνάω, συζητάμε ) or with B2 endings in more formal contexts ( τηλεφωνώ, συζητούμε ). When in doubt, B2 endings always sound more formal and polite for these crossover verbs. 🎯 Unit 9 Mastery Checklist I know that B1 and B2 verbs always carry the stress on their endings, not the stem. I can conjugate μιλάω (B1) and μπορώ (B2) in the present tense. I know that B1 verbs use -άμε and -άτε for 'we' and 'you all', and that the -άω forms are more conversational. I recognize that B2 endings look like Type A endings but carry the stress on the final vowels. I am aware that some verbs can "cross over" between B1 and B2 depending on formality. Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Routines & B-Group Verbs Up Next: Unit 10: The Subjunctive Mood (Intro to 'να')

verb-to-be.title: Unit 3: Being & Having
verb-to-be.content: Unit 3: Being & Having (Είμαι & Έχω) To build real sentences, you need verbs. In Greek, just like in English, the two most important verbs are To Be (I am) and To Have (I have). Together, they unlock hundreds of basic conversations. 1. The Actors: Personal Pronouns Before the verbs, we need to know the people. Greek handles "I, You, He" very logically. English Greek Pronunciation I Εγώ (e-gó) You (sing.) Εσύ (e-sí) He / She / It Αυτός / Αυτή / Αυτό (af-tós / af-tí / af-tó) We Εμείς (e-mís) You (plur./formal) Εσείς (e-sís) They (m/f/n) Αυτοί / Αυτές / Αυτά (af-tí / af-tés / af-tó) NOTE Rhyme Time & Grammar Tip: Notice that Εμείς (We) and Εσείς (You plural) rhyme! Also, the 3rd person pronouns ( Αυτός, Αυτή, Αυτό ) are highly versatile—they can be used as demonstrative adjectives meaning "this" (e.g., this man , this woman ) and follow the exact same ending rules as the nouns you learned in Unit 2! TIP Pronunciation Tip: The "Αυ" Vowel Twin: When Alpha (α) and Upsilon (υ) team up, their sound changes depending on the letter that follows them. Before voiceless consonants like Tau (τ), Kappa (κ), or Pi (π), the combination 'αυ' makes an [af] sound, which is why αυτός is pronounced af-tós . However, before vowels or voiced consonants (like β, δ, γ, λ, ρ), it makes an [av] sound. In rapid, casual speech, the initial 'ε' of words like εγώ and εσύ is sometimes dropped if the previous word ends in a vowel (e.g., 'γώ , 'σύ ). 2. Conjugating the "Big Two" Είμαι (To Be) Used for identity and state (I am a student, I am here). Είμαι (I am) Είσαι (You are) Είναι (He/She/It is) Είμαστε (We are) Είστε / Είσαστε (You are) - Both forms are correct and widely used as alternatives . Είναι (They are) — Surprise! It's the exact same word as "He is"! Έχω (To Have) Used for possession and family. Έχω (I have) Έχεις (You have) Έχει (He/She/It has) Έχουμε (We have) Έχετε (You have) Έχουν(ε) (They have) - The optional (ε) at the end is very common in everyday spoken Greek . 3. The Pro-Drop Rule & The Formal "You" Speak Like a Native: In English, you must say "I have a dog." In Greek, because the ending of the verb clearly shows the person and number, the subject pronouns ( Εγώ, Εσύ, κτλ. ) become completely redundant. You simply say " Έχω έναν σκύλο ." Greeks only use explicit pronouns when they want to highlight a contrast or place special emphasis on who is doing the action. Natural: Είμαι εδώ. (I am here.) Emphatic: Εγώ είμαι εδώ! ( I am here!). The Plural of Politeness: Greek uses the second-person plural to address a single person in a polite and respectful manner. You should always use the plural forms ( Εσείς, είστε, έχετε ) when speaking to someone unknown to you (like a taxi driver or shop assistant), or someone who is older or of higher status. 4. The Power Combo With these two verbs and the Accusative case from Unit 2, you can already speak! Έχεις το βιβλίο; (Do you have the book?) Όχι, ο Γιώργος έχει το βιβλίο. (No, George has the book.) Είμαστε φίλοι. (We are friends.) NOTE Question Intonation: Notice that you don't need to change the word order to ask a yes/no question like in English (e.g., You have -> Do you have? ). In Greek, you simply use the standard sentence order with a rising intonation of your voice at the end of the sentence. 🎯 Unit 3 Mastery Checklist I can conjugate είμαι and έχω by heart, remembering alternative forms like είσαστε and έχουνε . I know when to use Αυτός, Αυτή, and Αυτό , and I know how to pronounce 'αυ' as [af] or [av] . I understand that I should drop the subject pronoun when speaking naturally. I know to use the plural forms ( Εσείς, είστε, έχετε ) when I want to be polite to a stranger or elder. I remember to put definite articles in front of people's names! Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Everyday Objects & Places Up Next: Unit 4: Intro to Verbs (Group A)

verbs.title: Unit 4: Regular Verbs & Negation (Type A)
verbs.content: Unit 4: Regular Verbs & Negation (Type A) You’ve learned the nouns; now it’s time to make things happen. Verbs are the workhorses of the Greek sentence. They change their shape to tell you who is doing the action and when. 1. The Anatomy of a Verb Every Greek verb is formed by the combination of a stem and an inflectional ending . Take the verb "to play": παίζω (pé-zo). The Stem is παίζ- (it carries the essential meaning of the verb). The Ending is -ω (it tells you the person, number, tense, and voice). Almost all Greek verbs in the dictionary end in -ω . This always means "I" am doing the action. Because the Greek verbal ending clearly shows variation in person and number, a subject pronoun becomes redundant and is usually dropped. 2. Verb Groups (The Conjugations) Before conjugating, you must know which "team" or conjugation a verb belongs to. Look at the accent mark in the dictionary form: Type A (First-Conjugation): The accent is on the last syllable of the stem (NOT on the -ω). Examples: πα ί ζω, κά ν ω, γρά φ ω, διαβά ζ ω Type B (Second-Conjugation): The accent is on the final vowel of the ending (-ώ or -άω). Examples: αγαπ ώ (αγαπ ά ω), μιλ ώ (μιλ ά ω) Type C (Deponent/Passive Verbs): These verbs have no active forms and end in -ομαι instead of -ω, but they are active in meaning. Examples: έρχ ομ αι (I come), γίν ομ αι (I become) In this unit, we focus exclusively on Type A (First-Conjugation) verbs . 3. Conjugation (Present Tense) To change the person (I, you, we...), chop off the -ω and add these endings: Pronoun Ending Example: διαβάζω (I read) (Εγώ) -ω διαβάζ ω (Εσύ) -εις διαβάζ εις (Αυτός/ή/ό) -ει διαβάζ ει (Εμείς) -ουμε διαβάζ ουμε (Εσείς) -ετε διαβάζ ετε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ουν(ε) διαβάζ ουν(ε) NOTE Grammar & Pronunciation Tips: Fixed Stress: For Type A verbs in the active present tense, the stress remains on the exact same syllable throughout all forms (δια βά ζω, δια βά ζεις, δια βά ζουμε, etc.). The extra 'ε': The 3rd person plural form with the extra -ε (διαβάζουνε) is very frequent in the spoken colloquial language, but less often used in formal writing. He = They?: In English, "He is" and "They are" use different words. In Greek, for irregular verbs like Είμαι , they are the exact same word ( είναι ). But for regular verbs like διαβάζω , 'They' (-ουν) is distinctly different from 'He' (-ει). 4. Negation (Δεν vs. Όχι) To say "I do not," just put the word δεν (den) right before the verb. Δεν παίζω (I don't play) Δεν κάνω (I don't do/make) IMPORTANT Δεν vs. Όχι Use Όχι to answer "No" to a question. Use Δεν right before an indicative verb to say "not" ( Δεν καταλαβαίνω — I don't understand). Example: "Όχι, δεν παίζω" (No, I don't play). TIP Speak like a Native: Blending "Δεν" When the final /n/ of the negative particle δεν is followed by a word beginning with a hard consonant like π, τ, or κ , the sounds blend together! δεν + παίζω sounds like dem-bézo . δεν + τολμώ sounds like den-dolmó . δεν + καταλαβαίνω sounds like deng-gatalavéno . 🎯 Unit 4 Mastery Checklist I know that Greek verbs consist of a stem and an ending, which makes subject pronouns redundant. I can tell a Type A (First-Conjugation) verb apart from a Type B by checking if the accent is on the stem. I have memorized the endings: -ω, -εις, -ει, -ουμε, -ετε, -ουν(ε), and know the accent doesn't move. I know that δεν is used to negate verbs, while όχι is used for "No." Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Essential Action Verbs Up Next: Unit 5: The Accusative Case

vocabulary-action-verbs.title: Essential Action Verbs
vocabulary-action-verbs.content: A1 Vocabulary: Essential Action Verbs The most common Type A verbs to get you speaking right away. 1. The Big Two είμαι (í-me) = to be έχω (é-ho) = to have 2. Action & Interaction κάνω (ká-no) = to do / to make διαβάζω (dya-vá-zo) = to read γράφω (grá-fo) = to write παίζω (pé-zo) = to play βλέπω (vlé-po) = to see / to watch 3. Thought & Emotion καταλαβαίνω (ka-ta-la-vé-no) = to understand θέλω (thé-lo) = to want Practice Grammar: Unit 4: Intro to Verbs (Type A)

vocabulary-adjectives-comparisons.title: Adjectives & Comparisons
vocabulary-adjectives-comparisons.content: B2 Vocabulary: Adjectives & Comparisons Words for evaluating the world around you and comparing things. 1. The Essential Evaluators καλός, -ή, -ό (ka-lós) = good ➔ καλύτερος (better) κακός, -ή, -ό (ka-kós) = bad ➔ χειρότερος (worse) πολύς, πολλή, πολύ (po-lís) = much / many ➔ περισσότερος (more) λίγος, -η, -ο (lí-gos) = little / few ➔ λιγότερος (less) 2. Describing Size & Shape μεγάλος, -η, -ο (me-gá-los) = big ➔ μεγαλύτερος (bigger / older) μικρός, -ή, -ό (mik-rós) = small ➔ μικρότερος (smaller / younger) ψηλός, -ή, -ό (psee-lós) = tall κοντός, -ή, -ό (kon-dós) = short μακρύς, -ιά, -ύ (mak-rís) = long 3. Describing Quality νέος, -α, -ο / καινούριος (né-os) = new / young παλιός, -ά, -ό (pa-lyós) = old (for things) ωραίος, -α, -ο / όμορφος (o-ré-os) = beautiful / nice άσχημος, -η, -ο (ás-hee-mos) = ugly 4. Comparison Keywords πιο... (pyo) = more... (the easy way: πιο ωραίος ) από... (a-pó) = than... ( πιο ψηλός από εμένα ) ο πιο... (o pyo) = the most... (the superlative) Practice Grammar: Unit 25: Adjective Comparison

vocabulary-affection-emotion.title: Affection & Emotion
vocabulary-affection-emotion.content: C1 Vocabulary: Affection & Emotion Vocabulary specifically tailored to practice Diminutives and Augmentatives . Greeks use these words constantly to show love, familiarity, or emphasis. 1. Terms of Endearment (Υποκοριστικά) Add your new suffixes (-άκι, -ούλα) to these base words to show affection! η αγάπη (ee a-gá-pee) = the love ➔ η αγαπούλα (sweetheart) η ψυχή (ee psee-heé) = the soul ➔ η ψυχούλα (sweet soul) η καρδιά (ee kar-dyá) = the heart ➔ η καρδούλα (little heart) το μωρό (to mo-ró) = the baby ➔ το μωράκι (little baby) ο φίλος (o fí-los) = friend ➔ το φιλαράκι (buddy / pal) 2. Everyday Diminutives (Το -άκι) Almost any object can take the -άκι suffix to sound friendlier or smaller. το σπίτι (to spí-tee) = house ➔ το σπιτάκι (little house) ο καφές (o ka-fés) = coffee ➔ το καφεδάκι (a nice, relaxing coffee) το νερό (to ne-ró) = water ➔ το νεράκι (a little water) το φαγητό (to fa-yee-tó) = food ➔ το φαγητάκι (a nice meal) 3. Augmentatives (Μεγεθυντικά) When things are unusually large, impressive, or scary! ο σκύλος (o skí-los) = dog ➔ ο σκύλαρος (huge dog) το αυτοκίνητο (to af-to-kí-nee-to) = car ➔ ο αυτοκινητάρας (impressive car) ο άντρας (o án-dras) = man ➔ ο άντραρος (big man) η φωνή (ee fo-ní) = voice ➔ η φωνάκλα (loud, booming voice) Practice Grammar: Unit 29: Diminutives

vocabulary-ancient-phrases.title: Ancient Phrases in Modern Greek
vocabulary-ancient-phrases.content: C2 Vocabulary: Ancient Phrases in Modern Greek Modern Greek is heavily sprinkled with direct survivals from Ancient Greek. These phrases are used in formal settings, the news, and even everyday conversation to sound educated or poetic. 1. Expressions of Time & Status εν τω μεταξύ (en to me-tax-í) = in the meantime / meanwhile καταρχάς / καταρχήν (ka-tar-hás) = first of all / to begin with εκ των προτέρων (ek ton pro-té-ron) = in advance (e.g. "thank you in advance") εκ των υστέρων (ek ton ees-té-ron) = in hindsight / after the fact τουλάχιστον (too-lá-hees-ton) = at least (Note: this is an ancient superlative!) 2. Expressions of Agreement & Condition δόξα τω Θεώ (dóx-a to the-ó) = thank God (literally: glory to God) παρεμπιπτόντως (pa-rem-peep-tón-dos) = by the way / incidentally ως επί το πλείστον (os e-pí to plís-ton) = for the most part / mostly επί τούτου (e-pí toó-too) = on purpose / intentionally λόγου χάρη (ló-goo há-ree) = for example / "for the sake of the word" 3. Academic & Formal Nouns (The -σις / -ση group) These feminine nouns originally ended in -σις in ancient Greek. η λύση / λύσις (ee lí-see) = the solution η κρίση / κρίσις (ee krí-see) = the crisis / judgment η γνώση / γνώσις (ee gnó-see) = the knowledge η δήλωση / δήλωσις (ee dí-lo-see) = the statement / declaration

vocabulary-beliefs-society.title: Beliefs, Relations & Society
vocabulary-beliefs-society.content: B2 Vocabulary: Beliefs, Relations & Society Vocabulary designed to pair with Relative/Reflexive pronouns and advanced interpersonal discussions. 1. Reflexives & Relations ο εαυτός μου (o e-af-tós moo) = myself (used constantly in B2!) ο ένας τον άλλον (o é-nas ton ál-lon) = each other / one another η σχέση (ee shé-see) = the relationship το πρόβλημα (to pró-vlee-ma) = the problem ο κόσμος (o kós-mos) = the world / the "people" 2. Expressing Beliefs & Abstract Verbs πιστεύω (pis-tév-o) = to believe νομίζω (no-mí-zo) = to think (as an opinion: Νομίζω ότι... ) συμφωνώ (sim-fo-nó) = to agree διαφωνώ (dya-fo-nó) = to disagree ελπίζω (el-pí-zo) = to hope αλλάζω (a-lá-zo) = to change 3. Abstract Nouns for Conversation η ζωή (ee zo-í) = the life η αλήθεια (ee a-lí-thya) = the truth το ψέμα (to psé-ma) = the lie η ιδέα (ee ee-dé-a) = the idea (Plural: οι ιδέες ) η δουλειά (ee doo-lyá) = the work / job (Plural: οι δουλειές ) Practice Grammar: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns

vocabulary-colors-numbers.title: Colors & Numbers
vocabulary-colors-numbers.content: A1 Vocabulary: Colors & Numbers Describing quantities and colors. 1. Numbers 1-10 (Αριθμοί) ένα (é-na) = one δύο (dí-o) = two τρία (trí-a) = three τέσσερα (tés-se-ra) = four πέντε (pén-de) = five έξι (é-xi) = six επτά (ep-tá) = seven οκτώ (ok-tó) = eight εννέα (en-né-a) = nine δέκα (dé-ka) = ten 2. Basic Colors (Χρώματα) κόκκινος (kók-ki-nos) = red μαύρος (máv-ros) = black πράσινος (prá-si-nos) = green μπλε (ble) = blue ροζ (roz) = pink καφέ (ka-fé) = brown Practice Grammar: Unit 6: Adjectives & Agreement

vocabulary-compound-everyday.title: Common Compound Words
vocabulary-compound-everyday.content: C1 Vocabulary: Common Compound Words Greek loves to smash words together. Here are some of the most common compound words you will encounter every day, blending two concepts into a single word. 1. Time & Calendar Blends το Σαββατοκύριακο (to sa-va-to-kí-rya-ko) = the weekend (Saturday + Sunday) το μερόνυχτο (to me-ró-neeh-to) = a 24-hour period (day + night) το ημερολόγιο (to ee-me-ro-ló-yee-o) = the calendar / diary (day + word/logic) 2. Objects & Places το μαχαιροπίρουνο (to ma-he-ro-pí-roo-no) = cutlery (knife + fork) το αλατοπίπερο (to a-la-to-pí-pe-ro) = salt and pepper set το βιβλιοπωλείο (to vee-vli-o-po-lí-o) = the bookstore (book + to sell) το ανθοπωλείο (to an-tho-po-lí-o) = the florist (flower + to sell) το ζαχαροπλαστείο (to za-ha-ro-plas-tí-o) = the pastry shop (sugar + to mold) 3. Descriptive Compounds ο καλοκάγαθος (o ka-lo-ká-ga-thos) = good-hearted / kind (good + good) ο στενοκέφαλος (o ste-no-ké-fa-los) = narrow-minded / stubborn (narrow + head) ο χαμογελαστός (o ha-mo-ye-las-tós) = smiling (ground / low + laugh) η καλημέρα (ee ka-lee-mé-ra) = the good morning greeting (good + day) 4. Work & Society η πολυκατοικία (ee po-lee-ka-tee-kí-a) = apartment building (many + residence) η τηλεόραση (ee tee-le-ó-ra-see) = the television (far + vision) το λεωφορείο (to le-o-fo-rí-o) = the bus (people + carrier)

vocabulary-deponent-states.title: Deponent Verbs & States
vocabulary-deponent-states.content: B1 Vocabulary: Deponent Verbs & States Master the most common -ομαι verbs that describe thoughts, feelings, and body states. 1. Thoughts & Memories σκέφτομαι (ské-fto-me) = to think / to consider θυμάμαι (thee-má-me) = to remember ξεχνιέμαι (xe-hnyé-me) = to be forgotten / to get distracted αισθάνομαι (es-thá-no-me) = to feel 2. Daily Routines (Reflexive actions) κοιμάμαι (kee-má-me) = to sleep σηκώνομαι (see-kó-no-me) = to get up / stand up κάθομαι (ká-tho-me) = to sit πλένομαι (plé-no-me) = to wash oneself λούζομαι (loo-zo-me) = to wash one's hair 3. Interaction έρχομαι (ér-ho-me) = to come (Aorist: ήρθα ) γίνομαι (yí-no-me) = to become / to happen χρειάζομαι (hree-á-zo-me) = to need δανείζομαι (da-ní-zo-me) = to borrow Practice Grammar: Unit 18: Passive & Deponent Verbs

vocabulary-directions-commands.title: Directions & Commands
vocabulary-directions-commands.content: B1 Vocabulary: Directions & Commands Combine these words with the Imperative Mood and Adverbs to navigate the world. 1. Navigating the City (Αξιοθέατα) ο δρόμος (o dró-mos) = the road / street το ξενοδοχείο (to xe-no-do-hí-o) = the hotel το φαρμακείο (to far-ma-kí-o) = the pharmacy το νοσοκομείο (to no-so-ko-mí-o) = the hospital το πάρκο (to pár-ko) = the park το αεροδρόμιο (to a-e-ro-dró-mee-o) = the airport 2. Giving Directions (Imperatives) Πήγαινε / Πηγαίνετε! (pí-ye-ne) = Go! (Singular / Plural) Στρίψε / Στρίψτε! (stríp-se) = Turn! Προχώρα / Προχωρήστε! (pro-hó-ra) = Go ahead / Move forward! Σταμάτα / Σταματήστε! (sta-má-ta) = Stop! 3. Directional Adverbs δεξιά (dex-yá) = right αριστερά (a-ris-te-rá) = left ευθεία (ef-thí-a) = straight ahead απέναντι (a-pé-nan-di) = opposite / across δίπλα σε (díp-la) = next to Practice Grammar: Unit 16: Imperative Mood

vocabulary-everyday-objects.title: Everyday Objects & Places
vocabulary-everyday-objects.content: A1 Vocabulary: Everyday Objects & Places Words for the things and places around you. 1. Top Objects το βιβλίο (to vee-vlí-o) = book η τσάντα (ee tsán-da) = bag η πόρτα (ee pór-ta) = door το αυτοκίνητο (to af-to-kí-nee-to) = car 2. Food & Drink το νερό (to ne-ró) = water ο καφές (o ka-fés) = coffee η ζάχαρη (ee zá-ha-ree) = sugar 3. Places & Environment το σπίτι (to spí-tee) = house / home η οδός (ee o-dós) = street το σινεμά (to see-ne-má) = cinema ο ουρανός (o oo-ra-nós) = sky η θάλασσα (ee thá-las-sa) = sea Practice Grammar: Unit 5: The Accusative Case

vocabulary-family-people.title: Family & People
vocabulary-family-people.content: A1 Vocabulary: Family & People Learn the essential words to talk about people and family members in Greek. 1. Immediate Family (Οικογένεια) ο πατέρας (o pa-té-ras) = father η μητέρα (ee mee-té-ra) = mother ο μπαμπάς (o ba-bás) = dad το παιδί (to pe-dí) = child 2. People (Άνθρωποι) ο άντρας (o án-dras) = man / husband η γυναίκα (ee yee-né-ka) = woman / wife ο φίλος (o fí-los) = friend (male) η φίλη (ee fí-lee) = friend (female) 3. Professionals ο δάσκαλος / η δασκάλα (o dás-ka-los / ee das-ká-la) = teacher ο/η γιατρός (o/ee ya-trós) = doctor Practice Grammar: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns

vocabulary-family-possessions.title: Family Tree & Possessions
vocabulary-family-possessions.content: B1 Vocabulary: Family Tree & Possessions Perfect vocabulary for practicing the Genitive Case (e.g. "The brother of the mother"). 1. Extended Family ο αδερφός / ο αδελφός (o a-der-fós) = the brother η αδερφή / η αδελφή (ee a-der-fí) = the sister ο παππούς (o pa-poós) = the grandfather (Genitive: του παππού ) η γιαγιά (ee ya-yá) = the grandmother (Genitive: της γιαγιάς ) ο θείος (o thí-os) = the uncle η θεία (ee thí-a) = the aunt ο ξάδερφος / η ξαδέρφη (o xá-der-fos) = the cousin 2. Belongings & Pets το κλειδί (to klee-dí) = the key (Plural: τα κλειδιά , Genitive: του κλειδιού ) το κινητό (τηλέφωνο) (to kee-nee-tó) = the mobile phone ο υπολογιστής (o ee-po-lo-yee-stís) = the computer ο σκύλος (o skí-los) = the dog η γάτα (ee gá-ta) = the cat 3. Question Words for Genitive Ποιανού είναι...; (pya-noo eé-ne) = Whose is it? (Masculine / Neuter owner) Ποιανής είναι...; (pya-nís eé-ne) = Whose is it? (Feminine owner) Τίνος είναι...; (tí-nos eé-ne) = Whose is it? (Formal / Generic) Practice Grammar: Unit 15: Genitive Case

vocabulary-food-restaurant.title: Food & the Restaurant
vocabulary-food-restaurant.content: A2 Vocabulary: Food & the Restaurant Vocabulary meant to be combined with the Simple Past (I ate) and Subjunctive (I want to eat) alongside plural objects. 1. Restaurant Basics το εστιατόριο (to e-stya-tó-ryo) = the restaurant η ταβέρνα (ee ta-vér-na) = the tavern το μενού / ο κατάλογος (to me-noo / o ka-tá-lo-gos) = the menu / catalog το πιάτο (to pyá-to) = the plate (Plural: τα πιάτα) το ποτήρι (to po-tí-ree) = the glass (Plural: τα ποτήρια) 2. Eating and Drinking Verbs (Aorist Stem changes) τρώω (tró-o) = to eat (Aorist: έφαγα / Subjunctive: να φάω ) πίνω (pí-no) = to drink (Aorist: ήπια / Subjunctive: να πιω ) παραγγέλνω (pa-ran-gél-no) = to order 3. Essential Foods το ψωμί (to pso-mí) = the bread το κρέας (to kré-as) = the meat το ψάρι (to psá-ree) = the fish το τυρί (to tee-rí) = the cheese τα φρούτα (ta froo-ta) = the fruits (usually used in plural) τα λαχανικά (ta la-ha-nee-ká) = the vegetables 4. Useful Phrases Τι θα πάρετε; (ti tha pá-re-te?) = What will you have/take? Μπορώ να έχω...; (bo-ró na é-ho...) = Can I have...? Practice Grammar: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem

vocabulary-impersonal-weather.title: Expressions, impersonal states & weather
vocabulary-impersonal-weather.content: B2 Vocabulary: Expressions & Impersonal States Verbs and nouns that are frozen in the 3rd person singular to describe states, rules, and the environment. 1. Impersonal Rule Verbs (The "It" Verbs) πρέπει (να) (pré-pee) = it is necessary (must) μπορεί (να) (bo-reé) = it is possible (might) απαγορεύεται (a-pa-go-rév-e-te) = it is forbidden επιτρέπεται (e-pee-trép-e-te) = it is allowed αξίζει (ax-í-zee) = it is worth it φαίνεται (fén-e-te) = it seems / it looks like 2. Weather & Nature (Τα Φαινόμενα) Weather is almost always impersonal in Greek. ο καιρός (o ke-rós) = the weather βρέχει (vré-hee) = it is raining / η βροχή = the rain χιονίζει (hyo-ní-zee) = it is snowing / το χιόνι = the snow φυσάει (fee-sá-ee) = it is windy / ο αέρας = the wind ο ήλιος (o eé-lyos) = the sun έχει ζέστη (é-hee zés-tee) = it's hot (literally: "it has heat") έχει κρύο (é-hee krí-o) = it's cold 3. The "Είναι + να" Expressions είναι κρίμα... (eé-ne krí-ma) = it's a pity... είναι λογικό... (eé-ne lo-yee-kó) = it makes sense... είναι δύσκολο / εύκολο... (dís-ko-lo / éf-ko-lo) = it is difficult / easy... Practice Grammar: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs

vocabulary-irregular-everyday.title: Irregular Nouns & Verbs in the Wild
vocabulary-irregular-everyday.content: C2 Vocabulary: Irregular Nouns & Verbs in the Wild To achieve true mastery, you must know the common words that completely break the standard grammatical rules you learned in A1-B2. 1. The "Ancient" Plurals (Irregular Neuter Nouns) Some neuter everyday words take an extra syllable (-ακ-) when pluralized or put in the genitive. το κρέας (to kré-as) = the meat ➔ Plural: τα κρέατα το τέρας (to té-ras) = the monster ➔ Plural: τα τέρατα το φως (to fos) = the light ➔ Plural: τα φώτα / Genitive: του φωτός 2. Unchanging Foreign Words These words, heavily used in daily life, never change their endings no matter the case or number. The only thing that changes is the article (ο, η, το)! το σπορ (to spor) = the sport ➔ τα σπορ (the sports) το κομπιούτερ (to kom-byoó-ter) = the computer ➔ τα κομπιούτερ το μπαρ (to bar) = the bar το ταξί (to ta-xí) = the taxi (Grammatically foreign words don't change, e.g. τα ταξί , but colloquially you will often hear native speakers say τα ταξιά !) 3. The Rebellious Adjectives The adjective "much/many" and "big" are famously tricky to decline. Learn these key forms: πολύς, πολλή, πολύ (po-lís) = much / many Plural Forms: πολλοί (masc), πολλές (fem), πολλά (neut) μέγας, μεγάλη, μέγα (mé-gas) = great / huge (from ancient Greek, now used alongside 'μεγάλος') 4. The 3rd Person "Frozen" Genitive Absolute Sometimes, Greeks use ancient participle constructions that seem disconnected from the sentence (Genitive Absolute). όντως (ón-dos) = indeed / in reality / factually όντος (ón-dos) = being (Ancient genitive participle) (e.g. όλων των πραγμάτων όντων... = all things being...) τρέχοντος (μήνα) (tré-hon-dos) = of the current (month)

vocabulary-particles-fillers.title: Conversational Fillers & Particles
vocabulary-particles-fillers.content: B2 Vocabulary: Conversational Fillers & Particles The little words that make you sound like a native. 1. The Greek "Um" and "Well" λοιπόν (lee-pón) = well... / so... δηλαδή (dee-la-dée) = meaning... / that is (to say)... βασικά (va-see-ká) = basically / actually ούτως ή άλλως (oó-tos ee ál-los) = anyway / either way 2. Expressing Doubt & Sarcasm άραγε (á-ra-ge) = I wonder... (used in questions) δήθεν / τάχα (dee-then / tá-ha) = supposedly / "yeah, right" μάλλον (mál-lon) = probably / I guess ίσως (í-sos) = perhaps / maybe 3. Strong Emotion & Wishes μακάρι (ma-ká-ree) = I hope / if only! ας (as) = let's / let it be ( ας πάμε = let's go) άντε / άιντε (án-de) = come on! / hurry up! αλίμονο (a-lí-mo-no) = alas / of course! 4. Agreement & Disagreement ακριβώς! (a-kree-vós) = exactly! φυσικά / βεβαίως (fee-see-ká / ve-vé-os) = naturally / certainly αποκλείεται! (a-po-klí-e-te) = no way! / impossible! Practice Grammar: Unit 22: Spoken Expressions

vocabulary-questions.title: Question Words & Pronouns
vocabulary-questions.content: A1 Vocabulary: Question Words & Pronouns Mastering how to ask for things. 1. The "W" Words Τι; (ti) = What? Πού; (poo) = Where? Πότε; (pó-te) = When? Πώς; (pos) = How? Γιατί; (ya-tí) = Why? 2. Chameleon Question Words Ποιος / Ποια / Ποιο; (pyos / pya / pyo) = Who? / Which? Πόσο; (pó-so) = How much? Πόσα; (pó-sa) = How many? 3. Pronouns & Particles εγώ (e-gó) = I εσύ (e-sí) = you (singular) αυτός / αυτή / αυτό (af-tós / af-tí / af-tó) = he / she / it εμείς (e-mís) = we εσείς (e-sís) = you (plural) δεν (den) = not Practice Grammar: Unit 7: Asking Questions

vocabulary-routines-hobbies.title: Routines & B-Group Verbs
vocabulary-routines-hobbies.content: A2 Vocabulary: Routines & B-Group Verbs Common Type B verbs (B1 & B2 "singers") and expressions used to describe habits and routines. 1. Daily Routine Verbs (B1 -άω) These verbs love the Alpha sound. Remember: use them for habits (Imperfect/Continuous)! ξυπνάω (xip-ná-o) = to wake up μιλάω (mee-lá-o) = to speak / talk φοράω (fo-rá-o) = to wear (clothes) πεινάω (pee-ná-o) = to be hungry διψάω (deep-sá-o) = to be thirsty αγαπάω (a-ga-pá-o) = to love 2. Essential B2 Verbs (-ώ) These are crucial verbs for requesting and giving permission. μπορώ (bo-ró) = to be able to / can ζω (zo) = to live τηλεφωνώ (tee-le-fo-nó) = to call on the phone λειτουργώ (lee-toor-gó) = to function / operate 3. Routine Nouns Combine these with verbs to describe your day. το πρωί (to pro-í) = the morning το μεσημέρι (to me-see-mé-ree) = noon / midday το απόγευμα (to a-pó-yev-ma) = the afternoon το βράδυ (to vrá-dee) = the evening / night η δουλειά (ee doo-lyá) = work / job Practice Grammar: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2

vocabulary-shopping-clothes.title: Shopping & Clothes
vocabulary-shopping-clothes.content: A2 Vocabulary: Shopping & Clothes Perfect for practicing plurals, adjective agreement, and object pronouns ("I want those ", "Give me it "). 1. Shopping Locations το μαγαζί / το κατάστημα (to ma-ga-zí / to ka-tá-stee-ma) = the shop / store η αγορά (ee a-go-rá) = the market το σούπερ μάρκετ (to soo-per már-ket) = the supermarket 2. Verbs for Shopping αγοράζω (a-go-rá-zo) = to buy (Simple Stem: αγοράσ-) πουλάω (poo-lá-o) = to sell (Type B1 verb) κοστίζει (ko-stí-zee) = it costs ψάχνω (psáh-no) = to look for / to search 3. Clothes (Τα ρούχα) Notice how many clothes are naturally plural, just like in English! τα ρούχα (ta roo-ha) = the clothes το παντελόνι (to pan-de-ló-nee) = the trousers / pants (Plural: τα παντελόνια) το παπούτσι (to pa-poo-tsee) = the shoe (Plural: τα παπούτσια) η μπλούζα (ee bloo-za) = the blouse / t-shirt το φόρεμα (to fó-re-ma) = the dress το μπουφάν (to boo-fán) = the jacket 4. Money & Descriptors τα λεφτά / τα χρήματα (ta lef-tá / ta hrí-ma-ta) = the money ακριβός, -ή, -ό (ak-ree-vós) = expensive φθηνός, -ή, -ό (fthee-nós) = cheap μεγάλος, -η, -ο (me-gá-los) = big μικρός, -ή, -ό (mik-rós) = small Practice Grammar: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives

vocabulary-survival.title: Survival Phrases
vocabulary-survival.content: A1 Foundations: Survival Phrases In this first vocabulary list, we focus on the words that will help you survive your first 24 hours in Greece. 1. Greetings (Χαιρετισμοί) Γεια σου (ya soo) = Hello / Goodbye (informal) Γεια σας (ya sas) = Hello / Goodbye (formal or plural) Καλημέρα (ka-lee-mé-ra) = Good morning Καλησπέρα (ka-lee-spé-ra) = Good evening Καληνύχτα (ka-lee-née-hta) = Good night 2. Politeness (Ευγένεια) Ευχαριστώ (ef-ha-ree-stó) = Thank you Παρακαλώ (pa-ra-ka-ló) = Please / You're welcome Συγγνώμη (see-gnó-mee) = Sorry / Excuse me Ναι (ne) = Yes Όχι (o-hee) = No 3. Basic Needs (Βασικές Ανάγκες) το Νερό (to ne-ró) = Water το Φαγητό (to fa-yee-tó) = Food ο Λογαριασμός (o lo-ga-rya-smós) = The bill (in a restaurant) η Βοήθεια (ee vo-ée-thya) = Help Πού είναι...; (poo ee-ne) = Where is...? Practice Grammar: Unit 5: The Accusative Case

vocabulary-time-calendar.title: Time & Calendar
vocabulary-time-calendar.content: A2 Vocabulary: Time & Calendar Essential vocabulary for dealing with time, predicting the future, and talking about the past. Notice how these words pair perfectly with verbs in the Aorist or Future tense! 1. Days of the Week (Οι μέρες της εβδομάδας) Note: All days are feminine except for Saturday (το Σάββατο). η Δευτέρα (ee thef-té-ra) = Monday η Τρίτη (ee trí-tee) = Tuesday η Τετάρτη (ee te-tár-tee) = Wednesday η Πέμπτη (ee pém-ptee) = Thursday η Παρασκευή (ee pa-ra-ske-ví) = Friday το Σάββατο (to sáv-va-to) = Saturday η Κυριακή (ee kee-rya-kí) = Sunday 2. Time Expressions (Εκφράσεις χρόνου) σήμερα (sí-me-ra) = today αύριο (áv-ryo) = tomorrow (use with Simple Future: Θα πάω αύριο ) χθες / εχθές (hthes / e-hthés) = yesterday (use with Aorist: Πήγα χθες ) τώρα (tó-ra) = now μετά (me-tá) = later / after πριν (preen) = before 3. Frequency (for the "Video" Continuous verbs) πάντα (pán-da) = always συχνά (see-hná) = often κάθε μέρα (ká-the mé-ra) = every day ποτέ (po-té) = never (used with δεν : Δεν παίζω ποτέ = I never play) Practice Grammar: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα)

vocabulary-travel-experiences.title: Travel & Life Experiences
vocabulary-travel-experiences.content: B1 Vocabulary: Travel & Life Experiences These words are naturally used with the Perfect Tenses (Have you ever...?) and Conditionals (If I go...). 1. Life Experiences Verbs ταξιδεύω (ta-xi-dév-o) = to travel (Perfect: έχω ταξιδέψει ) επισκέπτομαι (e-pis-kép-to-me) = to visit (Perfect: έχω επισκεφτεί ) γνωρίζω (gno-rí-zo) = to meet / to know (Perfect: έχω γνωρίσει ) δοκιμάζω (do-kee-má-zo) = to try / to taste (Perfect: έχω δοκιμάσει ) 2. Travel Nouns το ταξίδι (to ta-xí-di) = the trip / journey η χώρα (ee hó-ra) = the country το εισιτήριο (to ee-see-tí-ree-o) = the ticket οι διακοπές (ee dya-ko-pés) = the holidays (always plural!) το νησί (to nee-sí) = the island η θάλασσα (ee thá-las-sa) = the sea 3. The "Experience" Adverbs όταν (ó-tan) = when (not for questions; used for "when I was there...") αν (an) = if (the key to Conditionals!) ήδη (í-dee) = already ( Το έχω ήδη κάνει - I have already done it) ακόμα (a-kó-ma) = yet / still ( Δεν το έχω δει ακόμα - I haven't seen it yet) Practice Grammar: Unit 20: The Perfect Tenses

voices-moods.title: Unit 19: Voices, Moods & Special Forms
voices-moods.content: Unit 19: Voices, Moods & Special Forms Greek verbs are highly inflected, meaning they change their endings to express a complex system of grammatical categories. Today, we review two of the most important concepts—Voices and Moods—and introduce the "Special Forms" of the Greek verb. 1. Voices (Οι Φωνές) Voice is the grammatical category that indicates the relationship between the subject and the action of the verb. Ενεργητική Φωνή (Active Voice): The subject is the initiator or performer of the action. Εγώ πλένω το αυτοκίνητο. (I wash the car). Η Ελένη ενοχλεί τον Μάρκο. (Helen annoys Mark). Παθητική Φωνή (Passive Voice): The subject undergoes or is affected by the action, or acts upon itself (reflexive). Εγώ πλένομαι. (I am being washed / I wash myself). Ο Μάρκος ενοχλείται από την Ελένη. (Mark is annoyed by Helen). TIP Reminder: The Deponent Verbs As we saw in Unit 18, not all verbs have both voices. Some verbs have passive endings but are entirely active in meaning. These are called Deponent verbs (e.g., έρχομαι - I come, κοιμάμαι - I sleep). 2. Moods (Οι Εγκλίσεις) Mood indicates the attitude of the speaker towards the action (whether it is a fact, a wish, or a command). Οριστική (Indicative Mood): Used for facts, reality, and making statements. It is characterized by the absence of the particles να or ας , and it is always negated by δεν . Το παιδί παίζει. (The child plays). Η Ειρήνη αγόρασε νέο αυτοκίνητο. (Irene bought a new car). Υποτακτική (Subjunctive Mood): Used to express wishing, hoping, expecting, planning, and possibilities. It uses the same verb forms as the Indicative, but it is introduced by the particles να or ας , and it is ALWAYS negated by μην . Θέλω να παίξω. (I want to play). Ας φύγει. (Let him/her go). Προστακτική (Imperative Mood): Used to give direct commands, orders, and requests to the hearer. Διάβασε! (Read!). Γράψε το γράμμα! (Write the letter!). 3. Special Forms (Participles & The Non-Finite Form) Greek verbs have a few invariant or special forms that do not behave like normal conjugated verbs. A. The Gerund / Active Participle (Η Μετοχή σε -οντας/-ώντας) This is an uninflected (frozen) verb form used as an adverb. It corresponds to the English "-ing" form when used to express manner, cause, or two actions happening at the same time. It ends in -οντας (if the stress is on the stem) or -ώντας (if the stress is on the ending). Ήρθε τρέχοντας. (He came running). Παίζοντας και γελώντας. (Playing and laughing). B. The Passive Perfect Participle (Η Παθητική Μετοχή) Unlike the gerund, this participle acts exactly like an adjective and must perfectly agree with the noun in gender, number, and case. It indicates a completed state resulting from an action. It always ends in -μένος, -μένη, -μένο . κουρασμένος (tired). Ένα καλά οργανωμένο συνέδριο. (A well-organized conference). WARNING The "Infinitive" Myth English relies heavily on the infinitive ("to play", "to go"). Greek does not have an infinitive . When you want to say "I want to play", you use the Subjunctive ( Θέλω να παίξω - "I want that I play"). Greek only has a Non-Finite form (Απαρέμφατο) , which ends in -ει and looks identical to the 3rd person singular of the dependent. It is never used on its own; it is ONLY used directly after the auxiliary verb έχω to create the Perfect Tenses! Έχω γράψει (I have written). Είχα χάσει (I had lost). 🎯 Unit 19 Mastery Checklist I understand the difference between Active Voice (doing) and Passive Voice (receiving). I know that the Indicative states facts (negated by δεν ), while the Subjunctive expresses wishes/possibilities (negated by μην ). I can recognize a Gerund ( τρέχοντας ) and a Passive Participle ( κουρασμένος ). I know that Greek has no true infinitive, and the form ending in -ει is only used to build perfect tenses with έχω . Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Abstract Concepts & Grammar Up Next: Unit 20: The Perfect Tenses
